methodologies for the evaluation at
market prices of the different Treasury
products (Fixed Income, Forward and
## Spot COP-USD), case statistics, among
## A wareness-raising initiatives on various topics
related to risk management aimed at all our
employees that include the development of
videos, infographics, series, among others,
which include questionnaires for the assessment
of knowledge and interactive activities.
## EThat is why each Risk Management, through
periodic meetings and a constant feedback process,
evaluates best practices and defines actions to
strengthen the risk culture in the bank, presenting
the results to the respective committees. In addition,
those responsible for each process are in charge of
implementing corrective measures together with their
teams every time an error is detected, thus ensuring
effective risk management, as well as:
## The Operational Risk Management organizes annual
training on the Operational Risk Management
## System (SARO) and the PCN for third parties that
provide services to the entity, while monthly training
is carried out for new members of the commercial
team. In specific cases, training is also provided to
risk managers or areas with particular needs.
## On the other hand, the Balance Sheet Risk and
## Treasury Management - Compliance carries out
detailed bimonthly reviews of the findings together
with the Treasury Management, analyzing the
recurrence of incidents and establishing action plans
to prevent future problems.
## Incorporation of Risk
## Criteria in New Products
## In accordance with the regulations of the Finance
## Superintendence of Colombia, the identification
of risks is carried out prior to the launch of new
products, services, or technologies, as well as
the incursion into new markets. The risk of money
laundering, terrorist financing and arms proliferation
is integrated into all our new projects through risk
analysis and the establishment of specific controls.
## The Balance Sheet and Treasury Risk Management
actively participates in the development of new
products, validating valuation methodologies and
evaluating the impact on risk metrics.
## This comprehensive approach allows risks to be
managed effectively and ensures that each new
project or product conforms to the principles of
prudence, control and regulatory compliance.
## At Banco de Occidente we analyze emerging
risks through the PESTAL methodology which
analyzes Political, Economic, Social, Technological,
## Environmental and Legal factors, through which
we were able to identify how climate change and
artificial intelligence and new technologies focused
on criminal activities, both in Colombia and globally,
can have a significant impact on the local economy
and that of our clients in the next 3 to 5 years.
## Emerging risk Category Potential impact Mitigation actions
TTaking into account that our bank customers belong to different
sectors, in the event of an effect generated by this risk, we may be
affected in the non-payment of obligations due to the damages
suffered, deterioration and/or interruption of operations.
Increased losses in portfolio exposed to sectors vulnerable to
extreme weather events.
## In addition, we may present an increase in market risk due to
the Bank’s lack of alignment with the sustainable preferences
Therefore, as an entity we seek to integrate climate risks with our
bank’s risk management model, with early warnings and mitigation
measures through which we can provide our customers with
better support and the satisfaction of their needs by reducing
environmental risks.
At the Bank, we have carried out a diagnosis of the impact of the
risk and we have a preliminary action plan which is being carried
out with the consulting firm Deloitte to update the diagnosis,
define the governance structure and develop the action plan
to mitigate the estimated potential impact.
## During the last 30 years we have valued more than 3,400
initiatives, encouraging the conservation and care of water,
the principle of life. Likewise, we continue with the development
of initiatives to mitigate and adapt to climate change.
Currently, due to the great scope that this risk has in our sector,
we can be strongly impacted by the theft of information through
applications, loss of money due to identity theft, theft of confidential
information through emerging technologies installed in frequently
used devices, kidnapping of information and extortion for its
This risk is thus reflected in the materialization of fraud, the loss
of customer information and confidential information of the
bank, thus leading us to the materialization of reputational risk
due to the impact on the service.
## We are working on the implementation of communication
strategies for customers and employees focused on preventive
actions and confirmation of authenticity in the face of possible
requirements through artificial intelligence, in addition we are
strengthening the authentication schemes for customers and
employees in channels and services provided by the Bank.
To mitigate everything related to the risk of new technologies
focused on criminal activities, as a bank we are using technologies
to protect against malware attacks on the devices of customers
and employees, we are also raising awareness about the risks
and threats to which customers and employees may be exposed
in the event of bad practices in the use of technologies and
implementation of cybersecurity tools that prevent attacks
from accessing the Bank’s infrastructure in cases of deception
## Our Credit Risk Management is carried out through
the Credit Risk Management System (SARC), which in
turn is part of the Comprehensive Risk Management
## System (SIAR), and includes the policies, procedures
and methodologies established for the proper
management of this risk in the bank’s portfolio.
## By December 2024, the total Bank portfolio totals
$48.99 trillion with an annual growth of 7.89% and
is distributed 67.64% in the commercial portfolio,
26.17% in the consumer portfolio and 6.19% in the
housing portfolio. The portfolio growth by type of
credit compared to December 2023 was: commercial
7.81%, consumer 5.81% and housing 18.75%.
## At the product level, the commercial portfolio is thus
composed of 70.64% by ordinary portfolio, 18.16%
by leasing, 8.43% by treasury, 1.73% by Unidirecto,
0.76% by Factoring and 0.29% by Credit Card. On the
other hand, in the consumer portfolio, the product
with the highest participation is payroll loans with
43.20%, followed by personal loans with 28.28%,
vehicle with 20.50%, credit card with 13.20% and
revolving credit with 0.32%.
## In general, the Bank serves customers in the
commercial portfolio of all economic activities, which
are grouped into 18 economic sectors where the most
representative are: Manufacturing industry with a
share of 16.48% ($5,460,847 MM) of the total portfolio,
followed by financial and insurance activities with
12.65% ($4,193,494 MM), wholesale trade with
9.86% ($3,268,675 MM),  retail trade with 8.09%
($2,682,212 MM) and supply of electricity, gas, steam
and air conditioning; water distribution; wastewater
evacuation and treatment, waste management
and environmental sanitation activities with 7.74%
($2,564,530 MM). The other 13 economic activities
represent the remaining 45.18%.
## For 2024, our commercial portfolio showed significant
growth driven in part by increased exposures in large
clients such as Enel, Ecopetrol, Almacenes Éxito,
Alcaldía de Medellín, among others.
## Regarding the financial system, we occupy the fourth
position in the commercial portfolio with a market
share of 9.3%, on the other hand, the commercial
non-performing loan portfolio presents a slight
upward trend compared to 2023, the average Non-

compared to 2.94% recorded in 2023. Regarding
the main banks in the financial system, we have the
second best commercial ICV, standing at 3.10% as of
## December 2024, which is 49 basis points lower than
the ICV of the banking system (3.59%). Regarding the
commercial portfolio scenario for 2025, we expect
the bank to continue growing placements in the
## Corporate, Government, International and Business

revolving and vehicles presenting a greater decrease
of 48.77% and 45.16%, respectively. The Consumer
## ICV in 2024 had a decreasing trend throughout the

of 38.53% compared to the end of 2023 (6.51%).

## Evaluation and Measurement
## In the granting of credit we have different models
for risk assessment with internal scoring models for
the evaluation of the consumer portfolio. In the initial
evaluation of operations, models are applied that
assign a score to the customer based on bureau
variables, as well as behavioral variables with the
sector that allow establishing whether the applicant
is subject to credit in accordance with the Bank’s
policies on the minimum score required. There are
monitoring models that mainly use variables of
customer payment behavior and use of products
that allow customers to be qualified and establish
the probability of default occurring during the next
For the commercial portfolio we have rating models,
whose variables are primarily financial indicators
that are obtained from the financial statements
delivered by customers, with these variables the
input models are obtained and for the monitoring
models, they are considered variables of payment
behavior. As described above, there are entry and
monitoring models for the segments of Industry,
Commerce, Services, Construction Companies,
Territorial Entities and Financial Entities. Among other
uses, these models are used in the credit granting
process for the classification of customers, in the
determination of the amount to be loaned, in the
structuring of commercial placement programs, and
in monitoring, for the qualification of the portfolio
and the estimation of provisions, as well as in the
monitoring of the composition of the portfolio by risk.
## Exposure limits by economic sector in the commercial
portfolio are monitored quarterly, where by December
2024, 100% of the sectors are within the established
appetite. In the consumer and housing portfolios,
monthly monitoring is carried out such as the Through
## The Door - Policy Monitoring Score by product and
the analysis of disbursements by variables, which
reflect the evolution of the harvests, from which
adjustments have been made in policies to improve
the performance of the portfolio.
## Management, Monitoring
## The credit risk models applied in the commercial
and consumer portfolio are monitored biannually
(June and December) in terms of the ability to
discriminate customers with good behavior and
bad payment behavior through Backtesting. The
## Accuracy-Ratio (AR) indicator is considered as a
measure of goodness of fit. In case the AR is at least
50%, the model remains in production, while if the
## AR is less than 50%, a new credit risk model must
## As part of the monitoring, in 2024 we carried out an
analysis of the impact of exchange rate risk on our
customers, showing that good behavior continues to
be observed in customers with an import vocation,
since capital presents a growing trend and despite
the increase in the overdue, the ICV is at very low
## Our portfolio in foreign currency increases its balance
a little and keeps the levels of non-performing loans
and risk low, due to this during 2024 we monitored
international behavior, with analysis of economic
growth and inflation, to understand the possible
effects and repercussions in the country.
## Similarly, we carried out national analyses, specifically
in sectors affected by situations such as the La Niña
phenomenon, government reforms, input prices,
among others; identifying the exposure in these
sectors, the number of customers and how the
dynamics of the portfolio could be affected. The
results of these analyses have led us to conclude
that exposure to customers and sectors with possible
impacts is low and controlled.
## Likewise, during the year we monitored the granting
policies by score, profile and risk nodes for each of
the products, where they have shown satisfactory
results with respect to the 2023 harvests, presenting
a decrease in overdue and non-performing loan
## We also made the cross-selling of credit cards and
payroll loans more flexible, as well as credit cards
and personal loans. Likewise, the youth credit card
had an adjustment, in which a decrease was made in
the cut-off points, among others. At the end of 2023,
the implementation of all products in the decision
engine was completed, with vehicles being the last to
be incorporated. This has allowed for a more timely
and efficient administration of granting policies, as
well as the administration of placement campaigns
and adequate monitoring of portfolio performance. In
addition, the implementation of the Decision Engine
leveraged a greater dynamic of Campaigns in the
Personal Banking portfolio.
## Country Risk Management
The country risk management system allows us to determine the need to have impairment provisions on
capital investments abroad. The foregoing corresponds to Banco de Occidente’s investments in Banco de
Occidente (Panama) S.A. and Occidental Bank Barbados Ltd.
In accordance with the provisions of the SIAR, in 2024 we carried out updates to the country risk assessment.
This study assigns a rating to countries in which capital investments are made abroad, which is composed
of a base analysis that includes financial, economic and market indicators, and a complementary analysis
that focuses on political, institutional, social and projection aspects, complemented by the ratings of the
risk rating agencies.
Given that our investment in foreign subsidiaries is in Banco de Occidente Panama - BOP and Occidental Bank
Barbados - OBB, entities through which we have portfolios and investments outside Panama and Barbados,
the country risk classification (A, B, C, D, E) of these two jurisdictions, in accordance with our policy, can be
improved by taking into account the weighted average of the ratings of the countries where the exposure
is located. According to the results of the latest update, the countries where we currently have investments
obtained the following rating: Panama and Barbados B.
Therefore, and following the policy established by the Bank, it is concluded that, according to the rating
obtained in both countries, no provisions are required for impairment of these investments.
## In accordance with the provisions of Chapter XXXI of
the Basic Accounting and Financial Circular in its part
II and part III regarding Operational Risk Management,
we manage operational and Business Continuity
risks through identification, measurement, control
and monitoring. In this regard, we are constantly
working on updating the risks and controls in the
processes, monitoring the risk profile and following
up on the record of operational risk events, which
allows the implementation of corrective actions
aimed at mitigating the materialization of events,
reducing the levels of exposure to operational risks
and the result of exposure to operational risk which
is part of the solvency relationship.

of this update, we had the following results in terms

areas, the evolution of losses due to operational
risk, action plans based on the materialized events,
among others. In the same way, changes in the risk
profile are reported, based on the identification of
new risks and controls in current and new processes.
## In addition, an annual training plan was developed
whose purpose was to strengthen the culture and
ensure understanding in adequate risk management,
thus strengthening the internal control system.

regulatory reports made by each area of the bank,
to comply with the established regulations.

## In accordance with the External Guide for the
## Supervision of Conduct Risk, issued by the Finance
Superintendence of Colombia on September 24,
2024, our bank implemented the Conduct Risk
## Management System, recognizing the importance
of properly managing these risks to protect the rights
of financial consumers and maintain confidence in
the financial system.
## Conduct risk is defined as the possibility of affecting
the rights of the financial consumer or the market,
derived from a practice of a supervised entity. In
this regard, our bank has implemented a conduct
risk management system, which includes policies,
procedures and methodologies designed to promote
fair, transparent and ethical practices in all our
operations, ensuring that our products and services
are aligned with the needs and expectations of our
## Since 2024, we have carried out the process of
implementing this system, continuously reporting to
the Board of Directors on its progress and evolution.
## To identify conduct risks, we carry out an analysis
based on the operational risk matrix and identify the
processes involved in the life cycle of our financial
products and services. From this analysis, we have
identified the conduct risks to which our financial
consumers are exposed and their potential impact.
## To this end, we consider the pillars of the Consumer
Service System (SAC) defined in Law 1328 of 2009,
the database of complaints filed by consumers in
the last year, and the classification of risk factors
and their causes established in the External Guide
for Supervision of Conduct Risk of the Finance
## As of December 2024, 139 risks and 526 controls were
identified. However, during 2025, a purging process
has been carried out to optimize the management
of these risks, prioritizing those with the greatest
impact on the customer.
## Finally, our bank promotes a customer-centric
organizational culture, supported by continuous
training programs for our employees, with the aim
of preventing possible effects and strengthening
trust with our users. These actions are aligned with
the commitment to mitigate the conduct risk.
## Business Continuity Plan
## During 2024, we continued with the work of
permanently updating the Continuity model (updating
strategies, process tests, business impact analysis
(BIA), and call tree), as well as monitoring compliance
with service level agreements for their respective
## Likewise, aiming to strengthening the business
continuity management system, we began the first
phase of implementation of the contingency scheme
under the Full VPN mechanism, where the employees’
house functions as a contingency operation center
for the Bank’s critical processes, this in order to have
an additional alternative to our current scheme and
thus have more tools in the face of crisis events that
## At the technological level, throughout 2024 we
executed activities on the infrastructure that supports
the Bank’s critical applications and we were able to
operate some applications in the Alternate Computer
## Center, guaranteeing that, in the event of a service
failure in the main computer center, the banking
operation would be continued.
## Finally, and in order to comply with External Circular
042 of 2012 of the Finance Superintendence, we
monitored the continuity plans of third parties that
provide us with critical services, a scheme that has
been strengthened with the support of Asobancaria.
## The Parent Company manages liquidity risk in
accordance with the standard model established
in Chapter XXXI (annexes 9 and 12) of the Basic
## Accounting and Financial Circular of the Finance
## Superintendence of Colombia and in accordance
with the basic principles of the Comprehensive Risk
## Management System (SIAR), which establishes the
minimum prudential parameters that entities must
implement in their operation to efficiently manage
the liquidity risk they are exposed to.
## To measure liquidity risk, the Parent Company
calculates a Liquidity Risk Indicator (IRL) on a
weekly basis for terms of 7, 15, 30 and 90 days, as
established in the standard model of the Finance
## Superintendence of Colombia and on a quarterly
basis for its subsidiaries to measure the liquidity risk
of the conglomerate.
## In addition, the Parent Company measures monthly
the stability of its funding in relation to the composition
of its assets and positions outside the statement of
financial position, over a one-year horizon through
the net stable coefficient (CFEN), as established in
the standard model of the Finance Superintendence
## On a daily basis, we monitor the availability of
resources not only to meet reserve requirements, but
also to foresee and/or anticipate possible changes
in the Parent’s liquidity risk profile and to be able to
make strategic decisions as appropriate. In this sense,
the Parent has liquidity early warning indicators that
allow establishing and determining the scenario
in which it finds itself, as well as the strategies to
be followed in each case. Such indicators include
the level of high-quality liquid assets, the levels of
concentration of deposits, the use of liquidity limits
of the Bank of the Republic, among others.
## Through the technical committees on assets and
liabilities (Finance and ALCO Committee) and the
## Comprehensive Risk Committee of the Board of
## Directors, the Bank’s senior management is aware
of the liquidity situation and makes the necessary
decisions taking into account the high-quality
liquid assets that must be held, the tolerance in
the management of liquidity or minimum liquidity,
strategies for granting loans and raising funds,
policies on the placement of surplus liquidity, changes
in the characteristics of existing products as well as
new products, diversification of sources of funds
to avoid the concentration of deposits in a few
investors or savers, hedging strategies, the results
of the Parent Company and changes in the balance
In terms of Liquidity Risk management, during 2024,
we had a sufficient level of liquid assets to meet
short-term liquidity requirements. In accordance with
the above, under SFC methodology, liquid assets and
30-day liquidity requirements stood at an average
of $8.6 trillion and $6.2 trillion respectively, yielding
a ratio of 139.3% that is comfortable with respect
to the appetite limit (minimum 120.0%) defined by
the Bank and well above the legal limit (minimum
100.0%). In ALM management, we highlight the
permanent monitoring of early warning indicators,
which generally had a stable behavior within the
established appetite limits.

assets as of December 31, 2024 and 2023 is detailed

## The management of Market Risk is mainly represented
by the management of the parent company’s fixed
income portfolio, which during 2024 stood at an
average of $8.89T, with an average duration of 45
months and a concentration of 46% in the medium
term (greater than 2 and less than 5 years). The
portfolio level is higher than that observed during the
previous year, due to the business strategy of having a
greater exposure of the portfolio to inflation given the
real rates presented by the market (UVR securities),
tactical purchases looking for favorable market
movements, as well as the dynamics of the market
maker scheme, clarifying that the latter portfolio is
hedged with derivatives of securities (put) and short
operations, closing the risk.

## Qualitative Analysis
## We participated in the monetary, foreign exchange
and capital markets in the search to satisfy its needs
and those of its customers in accordance with the
established policies and risk levels. In this regard,
we manage different portfolios of financial assets
within the permitted limits and risk levels.
## Market risk arises from the entity’s open positions in
investment portfolios in debt securities and derivative
instruments, from adverse changes in risk factors
such as interest rates, inflation, foreign currency
exchange rates and their volatility, as well as in the
liquidity of the markets in which it operates.
## At Banco de Occidente we negotiate financial
instruments with several objectives, among which
the following stand out:
O ffer products tailored to the needs of customers,
which fulfill, among others, the function of
covering their financial risks.
## S tructure portfolios to take advantage of
arbitrages between different curves, assets
and markets and obtain returns with adequate
capital consumption.
## C arry out operations with derivatives, for
intermediation purposes with clients or to
capitalize on arbitrage opportunities, both
exchange rate and interest rates in the local
and foreign markets.
## In carrying out these operations, we incur risks
within defined limits or mitigate them with the use of
operations of other derivative financial instruments.
## The risks assumed in carrying out operations are
reflected in a structure of limits for positions in
different instruments according to their specific
strategy, the depth of the markets in which they
operate, their impact on the weight of assets by
risk and level of solvency, as well as the balance
## These limits are permanently monitored and reported
daily to senior management, biweekly to the Finance
## Committee, monthly to the Risk Committee and
quarterly to the Board of Directors (Integral Risk
## Market risks are quantified through value-at-risk
models (internal and standard). The Boards of
## Directors approve a structure of limits based on
the value at risk associated with the annual profit
budget and establish additional limits by type of risk.
## We use the standard model for measuring, controlling
and managing the market risk of interest rates and
exchange rates in the treasury and bank books.
## These measurements are carried out on a daily basis
in the case of the Parent Company and monthly for
each of its subsidiaries to measure and monitor the
conglomerate risk.
## We currently map the active and passive positions of
the treasury book, within zones and bands according
to the duration of the portfolios, the investments in
collective portfolios and the net position (assets
minus liabilities) in foreign currency, both in the bank
book and the treasury book, in line with the standard
model recommended by the Basel Committee.
## Likewise, the Parent Company has parametric internal
management models based on the Value at Risk (VaR)
methodology, which have allowed it to complement
market risk management by identifying and analyzing
variations in risk factors (interest rates, exchange
rates and price indices) on the value of the different
instruments that make up the portfolios. These models
are JP Morgan’s Risk Metrics.
## The methodologies used to measure VaR are
periodically evaluated and subjected to performance
tests to determine their effectiveness. In addition, at
the Bank we have tools to carry out stress tests and/
or sensitization of portfolios under the simulation of
extreme scenarios.
## The entity’s senior management and Board of Directors
actively participate in risk management and control
through the analysis of an established reporting
protocol and the conduct of various Committees,
which comprehensively carry out monitoring, both
technical and fundamental, of the different variables
that influence the markets internally and externally
to support strategic decisions.
## Likewise, the analysis and monitoring of the different
risks incurred by the entity in its operations is essential
for decision-making and for the evaluation of
results. On the other hand, a permanent analysis of
macroeconomic conditions is essential in achieving
an optimal combination of risk, profitability and
## The ∆MNI has a short-term approach, since it
measures the impact of the shock scenario for the
one-year horizon and under the assumption of
constant balance sheet, i.e., that there is no growth
or decrease in balance sheet positions. This metric
reflects the impact on net interest income under
a parallel shock of +/- 400 bps. In addition, the
sensitivity for a parallel shock of +/- 100bps is
## The ∆VEP has a long-term approach and under the
assumption of balance sheet in liquidation, that is,
the total time horizon until the last maturity of the
positions on the balance sheet is evaluated. This
metric includes, under different scenarios, the change
in the present value of interest-rate sensitive assets
## Bank Book Interest
## Bank book interest rate risk is defined as “present or
prospective risk to the entity’s capital and earnings
arising from adverse movements in interest rates
and affecting bank book positions.” Likewise, Bank
## Book Credit Spread Risk (RSCLB) is defined as “any
type of credit spread and liquidity spread risk that is
not explained by the RTILB, nor by credit risk”. At the
## Bank, we have defined within our policies that this
risk is only applicable to bank book positions that
do not consume capital due to market risk, including
transactions of assets, liabilities and off-balance
sheet items that have this exposure.
## In this sense, we have exposure to interest rate
fluctuations that impact future cash flows. The risk
may arise due to the mismatch in the repricing time
between assets, liabilities and off-balance sheet
positions, the use of different interest rates (IBR,
DTF, SOFR, Fixed, etc.) and the options that may
generate changes in the cash flows of both the active
or passive positions made by the Bank (for example,
prepayments). Interest margins can be increased
or reduced as a result of changes in interest rates,
bringing with them impacts on the institution’s results,
however, at the Bank we have mechanisms such
as hedging through derivative instruments to deal
with the risks associated with the interest rate in the
## At the Bank, we manage the Bank Book Interest Rate
## Risk (RTILB) in accordance with the standard model
established in Chapter XXXI (annex 15) of the Basic
## Superintendence of Colombia, which establishes the
supervise in their operation to efficiently manage
To measure the RTILB, we calculated two indicators,
the ∆VEP (economic value of equity) under six shock
scenarios (parallel up, parallel down, flattening,
steepness, rise in the short, fall in the short) and the
∆MNI (net interest margin), under two interest rate
shock scenarios (parallel up and parallel down), as
and liabilities and, therefore, their final impact on
the economic value of equity.
## According to the regulatory framework, the SFC
requires that the maximum value of sensitivity to
the VEP under the six interest rate shock scenarios
be below 15% of the sum of the Ordinary Basic
Equity (PBO) and the Additional Basic Equity (PBA).
## Therefore, the Bank monitors compliance with this
indicator, and has a risk appetite statement, which
is monitored on a monthly basis.
## The results obtained in the measurement of sensitivity
to the VEP and the MNI for the cut-off December 31,
2024 are presented below, where it is evident that
the Bank is within the defined appetite (maximum
9.00% according to internal thresholds) and has
slack with respect to the regulatory limit (15.00%).
## In addition, the results obtained around the RSCLB for
the same evaluation period are recorded. Based on
historical information and for a scenario of interest
rate hikes, the Bank (for a three-month period) has
a probability of loss of $98.247B for investments
classified as available-for-sale that are not delivered
as collateral in central counterparty risk or in the
development of money market operations (repos,
buyback/sellback or temporary transfer of securities).
## The management of the RTILB, which
incorporates credit differential risk
and RSCLB liquidity spread, is the
responsibility of the ALM Management
and the Balance Sheet and Treasury

## Planning area and the commercial

keeping the RTILB within the defined
These results are based on the fact that the Bank’s cumulative reprice gap does not present a significant
mismatch and therefore the exposure to bank book interest rate risk (RTILB) is not significant when evaluated
from the sensitivity to the economic value of equity (VEP). When the duration of the asset and liability are
close, a change in interest rates affects both balance sheet items in similar proportions. This allows us to
conclude that maintaining a repricing structure with a not so wide mismatch is a way of protecting the value
of the entity’s equity.

In the case of the 2024 Stress Tests as regulated in Circular Letter 028 of 2024, four
scenarios were defined: the Base scenario, which corresponds to a context in which
the economy and the financial system do not suffer unexpected shocks; an adverse
scenario, which constitutes a hypothetical context in which the economy and the
financial system are affected by unexpected shocks which are severe enough to
produce breaches in the minimum regulatory requirements of solvency and liquidity;
an adverse scenario with climate risk (rainfall), which adds to the events of the adverse
scenario, the materialization of acute physical risks derived from climate change; and
an adverse scenario with mitigating factors, which preserves the macroeconomic
assumptions of the adverse scenario, but including the mitigating actions carried
out by the Bank to increase its solvency and liquidity levels above regulatory limits. It
should be noted that the adverse scenario is totally hypothetical in its construction to
model non-compliance with the regulatory solvency and CFEN (Net Stable Funding
Ratio) and is not foreseeable to occur in the medium and long term.
For this EPR (Stress Test Scheme) exercise that we carried out and in accordance
with what was defined by SFC (Finance Superintendence of Colombia), it is based
on information from balance sheet figures at end of December 2023 and is defined
as the basis for the projections for the following three years: 2024, 2025 and 2026.
Similarly, 32 variables are considered at the macroeconomic level for each of the
referred scenarios.
Regarding the Solvency Ratio, in the base scenario we comply in all the years of the
projection with the minimum requirements of Basic Solvency, Additional Basic Solvency
Ratio and Total Solvency Ratio, taking into account the implementation plan of the
conservation buffer defined in Decree 1477 of 2018. In the case of the adverse scenario,
we worked with a hypothetical scenario that would allow us to reach regulatory non-
compliance with the Total Solvency Ratio by 2026. At the beginning of 2026, the Total
Solvency Ratio was below regulatory levels, a situation that normalized in the mitigating
scenario and in which it was once again above the aforementioned limits.
## As for the CFEN, in the base scenario, the upward behavior of
the Available Stable Funding (FED) is offset by the increase in
the Required Stable Funding (RES), which leads to a stability of
the indicator and its regulatory compliance over the projection
horizon. In the case of the adverse scenario, there is a non-
compliance with the indicator at the end of 2025 mainly explained
by the bearish behavior of the Stable Available Funding (FED),
this situation is reversed in the adverse scenario with mitigating
factors, based on the Bank’s funding recomposition.
Additionally, and under the guidance received from the supervisor
under Circular Letter 028 of 2024, the Adverse Climate Risk
scenario is included through the construction of models that
allow evaluating the impact of the increase in rainfall on the non-
performing loan portfolio and the portfolio at risk, considering
the historical behavior from 2019 onwards. This effect was
incorporated considering the regional segmentation in which
the bank actively participates, and the initial conditions raised
in the adverse scenario were taken as a starting point.
## The Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing
## Risk Management System (SARLAFT) allows us to
comply with national and international regulations
on the prevention of financial crimes, protecting
its reputation and ensuring that it is not involved in
illicit activities. Among the functions exercised we
## C ustomer Due Diligence: It is important for us
to know our customers through the collection
and verification of detailed information, such
as identity, origin of funds, economic activity,
among others. We do this by assessing risks
based on the client’s profile, the transactions
carried out and their geographical location.
## T ransaction Monitoring: We will constantly
monitor transactions to identify unusual or
suspicious patterns that may indicate money
laundering or terrorist financing activities. This
can include large transactions, international
transfers, or movements that do not match the
customer’s profile.
## S uspicious Transaction Reports: When we
identify a suspicious or attempted transaction,
we must report it to the Financial Information
and Analysis Unit – UIAF, which is the competent
authority in the matter.
## Ongoing T raining: Our employees are regularly
trained to identify signs of suspicious activity
and understand the importance of compliance
with SARLAFT regulations.
## A udit and Evaluation: We conduct internal
audits to assess the effectiveness of the
## SARLAFT system, ensuring that all procedures
are in place and regulations are complied with.
## R isk Management: We identify and evaluate
the risks inherent to our operation, implementing
measures to mitigate the risks of money
laundering, terrorist financing and financing
of the proliferation of weapons of mass
destruction, this includes the segmentation
of high-risk customers, the control of specific
financial products and the implementation of
additional security measures.
## T he implementation of specialized transactional
monitoring scenarios was carried out, covering
both international operations and legal currency.
## This included the redesign and adjustment of
70% of the monitoring conditions for foreign
currency, as well as the definition of new
conditions for legal currency. The monitoring
conditions are expected to be fully operational
during the second quarter of 2025.
## T hrough technological modernization, the
massification of a unified consultation tool that
will integrate internal and external sources for
the bank’s different applications was achieved,
this will allow the implementation of process
automation with the aim of improving response
times. It is expected that the massification of
this tool will be fully available by the fourth
## T he percentages established by the regulator
were exceeded, achieving results higher than
those required in terms of the coherence of
the SARLAFT risk matrix, the detail of the final
beneficiary and the updating of customer
established by the regulator

## Global Systemically
## The Finance Superintendence in Circular Letter 74
of 2024 and in compliance with the provisions of
the paragraph of article 2.1.1.4.3 of Decree 2555 of
2010, published the list of systemically important
entities (SIE) for the year 2025, in accordance with
the methodology parameters for their identification.
## For the calculation of the indicator, information was
used with a cut-off June 30, 2024 and the results
were presented at Session No. 91 of the Financial
System Coordination and Monitoring Committee.
## According to the results, we were positioned in
fifth place, with a score of 6.60% against the
established threshold of 6.45%, so that for the
first time it is considered a systemically important
Bank.  This classification in no case represents a
perception or risk rating of the entities.
## Finally, it is important to note that the SFC, through
Resolution No. 2629 of December 27, 2024, granted a
period of 2 years progressively for the constitution of
the additional buffer of the systemic entity, equivalent
to 1% of the assets weighted by level of credit, market
and operational risk, as follows:

At Banco de Occidente, we reaffirm our commitment to sustainability and the responsible
management of environmental and social risks through the implementation of the
Environmental and Social Risk Management System (SARAS). This system was developed
in 2023 with the support of the NINT consultant, enabling us to define specific parameters
for  classifying and managing  these risks among ourBusiness Banking clients.
As part of the process, during the second half of 2023, we conducted a pilot test
involving 30 relationship managers, evaluating all their credit operations. Subsequently,
on January 31, 2024, we began the phased implementation of SARAS in three phases,
culminating in July 2024 with 100% coverage of SARAS evaluations for our Business
Banking customers.
The main objective of SARAS is to identify, mitigate and monitor environmental
and social risks in the operations evaluated, minimizing the Bank’s exposure
to regulatory, credit, operational and reputational impacts. The system
applies to all Business Banking operations, including Business 1 and 2,
Corporate, Government and the Structured Finance Unit (UFE), across all
sectors and industries.
## Risk Classification and
## SARAS classifies risks into three stages:
## We identify prohibited activities and
activities with exceptions due to their
environmental and social impact.
## We determine the risk category
## Follow-up and Monitoring
## We supervise operations with environmental
and social risk management tools daily.

## Integration of ESG Aspects
into Credit Analysis
## From the Structured Finance Unit (UFE), we integrate
ESG criteria into the analysis of large-scale projects,
particularly those related to construction sector. We
evaluate required permits and licenses and engage
in dialogue with clients to determine if environmental
and social due diligence is necessary. Before being
presented to the credit committee, all projects must
have term sheets reviewed by the UFE team, a project
engineer. No disbursements are made without the
required information and the above conditions met.
## Evaluation of Operations with
## Environmental and Social Risks
## By 2024, we evaluated 60% of the operations served
by the UFE, including two high-risk cases where an
environmental and social due diligence report was
required before proceeding with the evaluation.
## This evaluation is part of the third phase of SARAS
## We have an exclusion list that identifies
prohibited activities, and we establish
restrictions for certain activities with
exceptions, ensuring that they do not exceed
5% of the Bank’s portfolio.  For high-risk
transactions, we require environmental and
social due diligence reportsprepared by
external experts and reviewed by our senior
environmental analyst before submission to
credit approval committees.
## Oversight and Accountability
in ESG Incorporation

## R isk A (high): UFE operations in pre-construction
or construction, with amounts greater than USD
10 million and require an environmental licence
according to Decree 1076 of 2015
## Ri sk B (medium): Activities that may require
an environmental license according to Decree
1076 of 2015 and/or exceed USD 10 million.
## Ri sk C (low): Represents 98% of the operations
currently evaluated.

commitments with funders and annual reports of
results are presented. In addition, we make quarterly
reports to Aval in spaces with all the banks of the

## Equator Principles
Banco de Occidente is not a signatory to the Equator Principles. However, through SARAS, we assess
environmental and social risks in Business Banking credit operations, particularly those projects
managed by the UFE.
Since these projects typically involve high amounts and activities with a higher propensity for
environmental and social risks, the risk categorization form includes a section to assess the amount
and phase of the project. If a project is under construction or pre-construction, it is automatically
classified as high risk.
In 2024, we evaluated 60% of the operations served by the UFE, identifying two high-risk cases
where an environmental and social due diligence report was requested. We are still in the process
of reviewing and consolidating information to strengthen our capacity to analyze and mitigate
environmental and social risks.

## At Banco de Occidente, we embed environmental, social, and
governance sustainability at the center of our financial decisions.
Through our financing strategies, we promote the transition to a
more sustainable economy, strengthening the competitiveness of
our clients and generating a positive impact on the environment
With this perspective, we offer financing options that allow our
clients to incorporate ESG criteria into their economic activities
and projects. Not only do we have specific lines, such as Occiauto
Planeta Azul for the acquisition of hybrid and electric vehicles, but,
in essence, any type of credit within our portfolio can be framed
as a sustainable solution.
## PTo mobilize resources towards projects and assets
that generate environmental and social value, we
have developed our Sustainable and Transition
## Taxonomy, taking as a reference the Green Taxonomy
of Colombia, the taxonomy developed together with
IDB Invest and the Social Protocol of Asobancaria.
## Our taxonomy is structured in two key dimensions:

## Driving the Growth of Our
## Sustainable Portfolio
## At Banco de Occidente, we continue to strengthen our
commitment to sustainability through the financing of
projects and activities with positive environmental and
social impact. Based on our sustainable taxonomy,
we identified that our sustainable portfolio for
2024 reaches $3.61 trillion, of which $3.43 trillion
correspond to own portfolio and $173.262 billion
to development portfolio.
## Within our own portfolio, 25%  is allocated to
environmental initiatives, with an emphasis on
## Green Buildings and Renewable Energy,  while
75% corresponds to financing with social impact,
focused on SME owned or managed by Women
and Affordable Infrastructure.
## In line with our long-term vision, we have set out to
achieve a sustainable portfolio of $8 trillion by
2030. With the results obtained in 2024, we have
advanced 43%[2]  towards our goal, consolidating
our role as a key player in the development of a
more sustainable economy.
## In addition, we aim to ensure that the share of
the sustainable portfolio within our total portfolio
continues to grow year after year. Currently, this
segment represents 7% of our total portfolio, and
we continue to drive strategies to expand its reach
and impact in the coming years.
## Sustainable Finance
## Products and Services
## At Banco de Occidente, we recognize the critical role
we play in the transition to a low-carbon economy
and sustainable development. Through our offer
of sustainable finance products and services, we
seek to promote responsible investments, mitigate
risks associated with unsustainable financing and
generate value for our clients and society.
## Our portfolio of solutions is designed to support
companies, consumers and SMEs in the adoption
of more sustainable practices, facilitating access
to financial resources aligned with environmental
and social criteria. From credit lines focused on
energy efficiency and sustainable construction to
products designed to promote clean mobility and
social welfare, our strategy integrates sustainability
as a cross-cutting axis in financing.
## Below, we detail the main lines of credit and financial
products that reflect our commitment to sustainable
## Through our offer of sustainable
finance products and services,
we seek to promote responsible
investments, mitigate risks
associated with unsustainable
financing and generate value
for our clients and society.
## In our Green Constructor Credit line, we have
approved financing for $920.055 billion in 24
projects between 2023 and 2024, with disbursements
reaching $307.717 billion. This milestone reflects our
commitment to sustainability, aligned with Banco de
## Occidente’s strategy and support for projects that
promote a greener and more responsible future. We
seek to increase our participation in sustainable
projects, reinforcing our sustainability policy to reach
$248.828 billion in financing.
## One of the highlights is that the overdue portfolio
in green constructor credit line is 0%, which shows
the commitment of our clients to the fulfillment of
their financial obligations.
## These projects not only contribute to environmental
sustainability, but also generate tangible economic
benefits by optimizing the use of energy, waste,
and materials, aligning with the fight against
climate change. Financing sustainable projects
boosts economic growth and competitiveness,
strengthening the financial ecosystem. Likewise,
adopting sustainable practices allows for improved
operational efficiency and reduced costs, which
translates into greater profitability.
## With respect to Government Banking, we project
total disbursements of $1.1 trillion, of which $262.686
billion (24%) will be allocated to sustainable financing.

At Banco de Occidente we have put all our efforts into strengthening the End to End
(E2E) digital experience, which has allowed our customers to request and obtain
product approval in an agile and efficient way. Thanks to this strategy, we have
managed to digitize products such as Credit Cards, Personal Loans, Savings Accounts
and CDTs consolidating our commitment to innovation and process optimization.
An important milestone was the integration of the digital flow of Personal Loans
with the Bank’s traditional system, which eliminated the double filing of data and
significantly improved operational efficiency, this improvement will be massively
deployed in 2025 for the commercial team, and in the near future we will extend
this optimization to Housing and Vehicle products.
We also managed to launch the Digital CDT, allowing our customers to request it
in a completely self-managed way through our portal, without the need to go to a
branch, this advance is a milestone in the digitization of banking products and lays
the foundations for a similar flow in Housing that will be implemented in 2025.
In order to improve the experience of our customers, we have developed more agile
digital flows, a clear example is the optimization in the approval of Personal Loans,
which is now reduced to only two screens, making the process simpler for both our
current and new customers, by 2025, we hope to extend this optimization to Housing,
Credit Cards and Vehicles.
## T oken activation without having to go to the
## Implement ation of the Purchase of Debt with
## Credit Card and consultation of payment plan
for Vehicle Loans, PP and Payroll Loans.
In addition, we optimized the electronic signature,
generating annual savings of $30 million, and
launched the digital payroll MVP , becoming leaders
within Grupo Aval.
## These advances helped 66% of
our sales to be digital in 2024,
exceeding our previous goals, with
65% digitization in Personal Banking
and 55.2% in Business Banking.
## Segment % Participation % Participation
Semi - Massive 15.274 29%

## Digital sales have transformed the relationship with our
customers, providing more efficient, accessible and
sustainable channels, thanks to digitalization, we optimize
processes, reduce operating costs and expand our reach,
promoting financial inclusion and access to products and
services, all while minimizing environmental impact, this
evolution not only improves the customer experience, but
also strengthens our competitiveness and resilience. In
total, we managed to sell 109,506 units of various financial
products through digital channels segmented as follows:
P ersonal Loans: 14,583 approvals.
S avings accounts: 29,491 openings.
H ousing Loans: 1,573 approvals.
V ehicle Loans: 10,298 approvals.
C redit Cards: 51,011 approvals.
CD T: 12 openings.
## Allied  Portfolio: 2,538 openings of savings/checking
## These figures reflect the positive impact
of our digital solutions, which allow for
more agile and accessible management
of the products offered.
Below, we detail the percentage of participation of each digital channel in total sales:

by type of product and channel, highlighting that
products such as Vehicles and Credit Cards have
a high demand in digital channels, with approvals
of 72% and 66%, respectively, while Housing and
## Personal Loans are more requested in traditional
In 2024, 378,936 customers made digital transactions,
reflecting a 30.9% adoption of the bank’s platforms,
with an average value of $868,901. Customers with
income between $2 million and $4 million were the
most active, registering 15,274 operations.

through digital strategies.

through the bank's web platform.

and personalized interaction with
customers, making it easier to make
simple transactions and inquiries.

D igital channels were widely accepted,
especially for products with high margins
such as Personal Loans, with an average
conversion of 38% in all products, and up to
51% in Vehicles and Savings Accounts.

transactions, with a 91% success rate,

platform, The average value of transactions

## Customer Satisfaction
## We measured our customers’ satisfaction with digital
platforms using the System Usability Scale (SUS),
obtaining positive results, the transactional portal
achieved a rating of 4.7/5.0, while mobile banking
recorded scores of 4.3 on iOS and 4.4 on Android,
these results reflect a mostly satisfactory experience.
## Despite not having received recurring complaints
related to digital sales, we remain committed to
continuous improvement. The Customer Experience
team has not reported any significant negative
feedback that could impact the performance of our
platforms, confirming that digitization initiatives are
having a positive impact on customer satisfaction
and the service provided.
## In terms of performance by product, the most sold
through digital channels were the Savings Account
and the Personal Loan, the latter standing out for its
high conversion rate due to its flexibility and ease
## Vehicles and Credit Card products also showed
strong digital conversion, with 72% and 66%
share respectively, lreflecting customers’ growing
preference for making high-value transactions
through digital platforms. However, products such
as Housing continue to be in greater demand in
traditional channels, suggesting that the combination
of digitalization and face-to-face support is still key
for certain products.
## Customers with monthly income between $2 million
and $4 million were the most active in the use of
digital channels, making a total of 15,274 operations,
which shows the growing interest in the convenience
and accessibility that these platforms provide to
## This has been a key
year in our digital
transformation, with
a notable increase in
digital sales, a strategic
investment in marketing,
and a constant focus
customer experience.
## Looking ahead to 2025, we expect digital channels
to continue to play a critical role in acquiring new
customers and converting sales, especially in key
products such as Personal Loans and Vehicles.
## Continuous optimization of our platforms and
customer satisfaction analysis will be essential to
maintain and consolidate this success.

At the Bank we have implemented an information security model aligned with international standards
ISO27001-2022 and NIST 800-53, which is composed of policies, standards and procedures focused on
protecting the information of both our customers and the entity. These guidelines are documented, approved
by our senior management and are carried out within our organization.  We regularly review and update this
model to ensure that it remains aligned with the Bank’s technological advances, thus ensuring a high level
of information security and adding value to the commitments we have with our customers, shareholders and
the community at large.
In addition, we have an evaluation process in collaboration with the consulting firm Ernest & Young (EY), whose
objective is to measure our level of maturity and ensure that the implementation of the model is adequate
To comply with the provisions of our security model,
we have processes, services and tools designed to
control cyber risk and we have an incident response
plan that seeks to mitigate the impact in terms of
availability, confidentiality and integrity of services,
in the event of security or cybersecurity incidents.
## Regulatory compliance
and privacy audits
## We rigorously comply with Law 1581 on the Protection
of Personal Data, guaranteeing that the collection,
storage and processing of our clients’ personal
data is carried out in full respect of the principles
of transparency, legality and security. In compliance
with the above we carry out periodic audits in terms
of cybersecurity to evaluate the effectiveness of our
information leakage prevention (DLP) controls.
## Culture of information
security and cybersecurity
## We ensure that all our employeesare aware of the
risks and importance of information security and
cybersecurity, for this, we have implemented policies
and procedures that allow them to understand and
face possible threats.
## In addition, we carry out mandatory annual training
through the Success Factors platform, where
knowledge and good practices on information
security are reinforced. If any employeedetects
suspicious activity, we have a clear process to
report it, detailed in Procedure AVBO-PRSI-SI-002,
“Security Incident Report”.
## In 2024, we trained 5,667 employees through in
person talks and reached 5,467 more through the
virtual course, thus reinforcing our culture of data
protection and security.
## Although information security and cybersecurity are
not directly included in the performance evaluation,
we treat each incident with care, working together
with the Human Talent area to evaluate the impact
and take the necessary actions, including disciplinary
measures if necessary.
## We implement solid contingency and risk management
strategies, supported by a specialized team and
solid governance that involves those responsible
for security, cybersecurity, business continuity
and operational risks. In addition, at the time of
subscribing services, our clients are duly informed
about the current privacy policies, which reinforces
our commitment to the protection of their information.
In addition, we implement continuous awareness campaigns on the main cyber risks
to mitigate possible threats to information security.
To address these risks in a comprehensive manner, we have a Security Committee
that meets monthly, composed of the Vice President of Risks, responsible for the
areas of Information Security, Operational Risk and Business Continuity, and the Vice
President of Operations and Technology, Pedro Villegas, who fulfills the role of Chief
Technology Officer (CTO) and Chief Information Security Officer (CISO). In the same
way, he is in charge of supervising the Technology Management, which is responsible for
Cybersecurity in the Bank. At the meetings held, the initiatives in progress are reviewed,
an exhaustive monitoring of the risks identified and their mitigation is carried out, and
the incidents that have occurred are evaluated, which ensures robust protection of
information and operational continuity.  Both Vice-Presidents have extensive previous
experience related to their responsibility within the financial sector.
## IT security, cybersecurity
processes and infrastructure
We have established business continuity plans and incident response procedures,
which are tested at least twice a year Although we do not have certifications such as
ISO 27001 or NIST, we follow the ISO 27001:2022 framework, evaluated annually by
E&Y . In addition, we manage vulnerabilities using tools such as Qualys and apply a
due diligence process with our suppliers to ensure compliance with security standards.
In 2024 we received 4 complaints from regulatory bodies, which is a decrease from the
7 we received in 2023 and 2022, indicating an improvement in incident management
through the implementation of more effective internal processes that detect and
respond quickly to data breaches. This allowed us to contain the impact and reduce
the number of affected data subjects.

## We implement continuous
awareness campaigns on the main
cyber risks to mitigate possible
threats to information security.
## W e have achieved 100% of the
authorizations for the processing of
personal data of our active customers,
ensuring their consent in accordance
with the current clause, the authorization
for commercial management and the
permitted contact channels.
## W e carried out three trainings aimed at all
## Bank employees, ensuring that everyone
was aligned with the knowledge and
compliance with these policies.
## Integrating Artificial Intelligence and new
technologies in the management of personal
data, which will allow us to improve the
experience of our customers, managing
their data more efficiently, offering more
personalized attention and optimizing
operational efficiency through automation
in the management of this data.
In compliance with the provisions of Law 603 of 2000, Banco de Occidente has defined policies, controls and
sanctions to guarantee the legality of the software used. The controls for the acquisition, development and
maintenance of the software, in accordance with the legal requirements on copyright, privacy and electronic
commerce, are mandatory for compliance by the responsible areas. In this regard, the internal audit carries
out continuous monitoring to verify adequate compliance with the regulations.
On the other hand, in IT there is a licensing area that manages the SW that has an associated licensing
and for the SW Free there is a review and approval flow in which IT Architecture and Information Security
participates through the IT services portal. This review allows these utilities to be included in the bank’s utility
catalog and facilitates new requests.
We can confirm that since 2003 the Bank has had the information security model that includes the policies,
standards and procedures that define the information security governance that must be complied with by
employees and contracted third parties; in particular, the following definitions are available in the Information
## Policy: Compliance
## At Banco de Occidente we must comply with local and
international regulations on privacy and information
## The Information Security and Cybersecurity Policy
is in line with and supports compliance with local
and international laws and regulations regarding
information security. Therefore, such requirements
must be included in the development of the security
model and specific actions must be taken to keep
the Bank permanently aligned with such provisions.
Examples of such provisions are bank reserves,
software licensing, circulars from the Finance
## Superintendence of Colombia and those from
international groups such as the Basel Group for
the Supervision of Financial Institutions.
## In addition, and in order to maintain a
good level of security, this policy must
be based on best information security
practices, such as ISO 27001:2015
## Standard: Compliance
with legal regulations and
external regulations
## Any rule, regulation or contractual requirement for
each computing resource must be explicitly defined
and formally documented. There must be documents
that establish how Banco de Occidente’s computer
resources comply with any internal or external
regulations, rules or contractual requirements.
## Standard: Copyright
## The installation, reproduction, use and storage
of software, multimedia files and external
information in the Bank’s computer resources
must be previously authorized and must comply
with the legal requirements that enable their use.
## Appropriate procedures should be put in place to
ensure compliance with legal restrictions on the
use of material that may be subject to intellectual
property rights such as copyright and design rights.
## Standard: Installation of
authorized software
## The software that resides in the institution’s computer
resources may only be authorized by Banco de
Occidente. Software that is not registered and
authorized may not be installed or run on Banco
de Occidente’s computer resources.
## Minimum security and
quality requirements for
information handling.
## Currently, Banco de Occidente can confirm
compliance with the numerals required by the
## Finance Superintendence of Colombia in the face
of the latest update of External Circular 029 of
2019 chapter I “Channels, means, security and
quality in the management of information in the
provision of financial services.” It is possible to
maintain compliance with each of the requirements
defined therein and in particular with the use of
biometrics as a differentiating element in the process
of authenticating the identity of customers and
users, through the use of the technological solution
provided by the National Registry of Civil Status and
Certicamara for this purpose.
## Our corporate governance is essential to ensure
responsible, transparent management aligned with
the highest national and international standards. In
2024, we continued to consolidate our governance
structure with an ethical and sustainable approach,
implementing new measures such as the creation
of a Sustainability Committee that oversees our
ESG practices. In addition, we strengthened
transparency by publishing annual impact reports
and implementing independent external audits. With
these actions, we guaranteed the protection of the
rights of our shareholders, investors, employees, local
communities and other stakeholders, reaffirming our
commitment to an inclusive and responsible model.
## In this chapter, we present the practices, policies
and actions implemented in the field of corporate
governance, as well as the results obtained, in
coherence with the principles of transparency, integrity
and responsibility that guide our management.
## Through this approach, we seek to strengthen our
position as a solid financial institution, committed to
the economic and social development of the country,
while generating long-term sustainable value.
## In 2024 we elaborated the Corporate Governance
## Report for 2023, which seeks to reflect how through
the implementation of the 148 recommendations of
the “Country Code” we ensure transparency, ethics
and accountability in companies. At the Bank we
achieved an adoption rate of 87.3% (implementation
of 130 recommendations) which shows our
commitment to best practices in governance, This
not only improves the confidence of investors and
other stakeholders, but also promotes a strong and
responsible organizational culture.
## Our corporate governance is essential
to ensure responsible, transparent
management aligned with the highest
national and international standards.

## We held the Shareholders Assembly on March
20, 2024 in person at the Bank’s registered office,
located in Cali (Colombia). The meeting was held
in accordance with the applicable regulatory
provisions, as well as the Bylaws, the Regulations
of the General Shareholders Assembly and other
internal regulations of the Bank. During the event,
shareholders were provided with all the information
necessary for decision-making. Both our Presidency
and the Assembly had a participation of 148,745,581
shares represented out of a total of 155,899,719
subscribed and paid, that is, the percentage of
participation was 95.41%.
## The measures adopted during the year to
encourage shareholder participation were a direct
communication by the President  and Secretary of
## Regarding the information or requests submitted
by shareholders, the management of the previous
president Mr. Cesar Prado Villegas was exalted
and the work carried out by the current president
Mr. Gerardo Silva in the months he had been in the
## Presidency was exalted, they also mentioned that
they were proud that the Bank was managing ESG
and sustainability issues, actions that will allow the
## Bank to position itself and make its presence more
profitable in front of investors.
## The Assembly developed a precise agenda in
accordance with the legislation in force on the matter.
## Among the main agreements made are: approval of
## Financial Statements, election of the members of the
## Board of Directors and their remuneration; Election
of the Main and Alternate Statutory Auditor; the
approval of the Profit Distribution Proposal; Approval
of the Bylaw Reform related to the composition of
the Board of Directors, among others.
## With respect to the tax audit, in 2024 the firm KPMG
S.A.S was involved, for which the Assembly approved
fees for a value of $3.618B.
## Finally, during 2024, requests were received from
shareholders, related to the payment of dividends,
transfers of shares, request for tax certificates, among
others, which were fully answered.
## During the event, shareholders were provided with all
the information necessary for decision-making.
## During the period from January to March 2024, the
## Board of Directors was composed of five (05) main
members and five (05) alternates. At the General
## Shareholders Assembly, we approved a bylaw reform
that modified the structure of the Board, leaving it
made up for the period from March 2024 to March
2025 by nine (09) members, without alternates. As
established in the Bylaws, the deliberative quorum is
made up of the presence of the majority of Directors.
## At the Bank we have three Support Committees of
the Board of Directors:
## A udit Committee: this committee is in charge
of generating reports based on transparency
and detecting opportunities for improvement
that contribute to the optimization of processes
and is carried out quarterly.
## It is composed of three (3) members of the Board
of Directors who have experience, and know,
among others, accounting, financial and other
matters associated with the functions assigned
to the Audit Committee and are independent.
## During the period covered by the report, nine
(09) sessions were held, of which seven (07)
were regular and two (02) special.
## C omprehensive Risk Committee: is responsible
for monitoring the result of the risk profile and
appetite, advising the Board of Directors on
operations, events or activities, including the
incursion into new ones, that may affect the
entity’s exposure and risk profile, approving
adjustments and changes to SIAR policies,
monitoring the state of the risk culture in the
entity,  evaluating the analysis of the results of
the monthly risk management reports, among
others. It is also responsible for promoting the
adaptation of risk management in society to an
advanced model that allows the configuration
of a risk profile in accordance with strategic
objectives, validating reviews of the positive
and negative impacts generated by social and
environmental issues,  supervising compliance
with the comprehensive management of
climate change risk and being present in the
accountability of the SARAS Environmental
and Social Risk Management System. It is held
## It is composed of eleven (11) members, of which
nine (9) are members of the board of directors,
at least one of them independent, the other
two (2) members are the President and the Vice
President of Risk and Collections of the Bank.
## During 2024, three (03) regular sessions were
## A ppointments, Remuneration and Corporate
## Governance Committee: it is the one that
supervises and manages various key areas,
related to the management of high-level
personnel, salary policies and the governance
structure of the entity, within its functions are:
## P ropose or review appointments of
key managers and members of senior
management, as well as other strategic
positions within the Bank.
## E nsure that remuneration is aligned
with the entity’s strategic objectives,
incentivizing adequate performance and
responsible management.
## E valuate and propose improvements to
the governance structure, including the
composition of the board of directors
and internal committees.
## This committee is held quarterly and is composed
of five (5) members appointed by the Board of
## Directors, of which three (3) are members of the Board
of Directors, most of them independent, the other
two (2) are the President of the Bank and the Legal
## Vice President with their alternates Vice President
of Companies and Vice President of Human and
Administrative Talent, respectively. Three (03) regular
sessions were held of this committee.
## The inclusion of independent members in committees
is essential to ensure effective governance and
impartiality in decision-making, these members,
having no direct ties to the organization, bring an
objective and disinterested perspective, which allows
situations to be evaluated in a more equitable and
transparent manner. Their independence ensures
that decisions are made without external influences
or conflicts of interest, which strengthens internal
control, fosters shareholder and investor confidence,
and facilitates compliance with best governance
practices and regulations. In this way, they contribute
to the proper functioning of the Bank by protecting
its long-term interests and promoting ethical and
appointment process
## The process for appointing the members of the Board
of Directors was carried out in accordance with the
provisions of the Bylaws, and was carried out during
the General Shareholders Assembly on March 20,
2024. In that Assembly, the electoral quotient system
was applied, which consists of dividing the number
of votes obtained by each candidacy by the total
number of seats available in the constituency, a
key mechanism for the proportional allocation of
seats. For the statutory period between March 2024
and 2025, the Board of Directors was formed in
accordance with the lists presented by Grupo Aval
Acciones y Valores S.A., of which seven (07) lines are
independent, thus ensuring adequate representation
and balance within the governing body.
## Subsequently, the legal procedures to take office were
carried out before the Finance Superintendence of
Colombia, which were duly approved.  This process,
carried out with total transparency, reinforces the
reliability of the decisions taken and ensures that the
governance structure is aligned with the country’s
regulations, providing our shareholders and investors
with the certainty that we are committed to best
practices and compliance with current legislation.

## Changes in the Board of
## Directors during the Fiscal Year
## At the General Shareholders Assembly held on March
20, 2024, taking into account the list presented by
Grupo Aval Acciones y Valores S.A., the following
## Board of Directors was elected with the favorable
vote of 94.40% of the stock capital for the period
process considers various aspects relevant to the
development of the organization. In accordance with
## Article 28 of the bank’s bylaws, it is the responsibility
of the Shareholders Assembly to freely appoint and
remove the members of the Board of Directors,
accept their resignations and fill vacancies. This
process is carried out after a thorough review by
the Appointments, Remuneration and Corporate
## Governance Committee, which assesses the suitability
of the candidates and their independence rating.
The Board of Directors, or shareholders directly,
present the proposals to the General Shareholders
## Assembly, ensuring that fundamental aspects such
as stakeholder opinions, diversity, independence and
the competencies necessary to address the Bank’s
impacts are taken into account.
## Independence criterion
## Board members or applicants must declare that
## E mployees or directors of the Bank or of any of its
affiliates, subsidiaries or controlling companies,
and have not held such status during the year
immediately prior to this appointment.
## S hareholders who directly or by virtue of an
agreement direct, guide or control the majority
of the Bank’s voting rights or who determine
the majority composition of the Bank’s
administrative, management or control bodies.
## A  member or employees of associations or
companies that provide advisory or consulting
services to the Bank or of companies belonging
to the same economic group of which it is a part,
when the income from such concept represents
twenty percent (20%) or more of their operating
E mployees or directors of a foundation,

donations from the Bank (Important donations

received by the respective institution).

## Functions of the Board
## Our Board of Directors meets biweekly, and during
the period covered in this report, policies, guidelines,
and decisions related to the social, economic, and
environmental impact of the Bank’s operations on
our stakeholders were approved.
## Among the functions of the Board of Directors is
to present to the General Shareholders Assembly,
together with the Bank’s senior management, the
reports and balance sheets for each year and the
profit distribution proposals, accompanied by a report
on the progress of the business and general situation
of the entity, within which there is a sustainability
chapter with the information reported herein.
## During 2024, all the regular sessions scheduled for
the year were held, and 4 additional sessions that
were held in a special manner. The Board of Directors
has held four (04) special sessions.

## Vice-presidency Vice-president
## Industrial Engineer and MBA
## Bank Experience: 6 years
## Previous experience at Grupo Aval: 12 years
Guides and directs the financial policies and procedures that allow the fulfillment of planning objectives
Budgeting, financial analysis, pricing, profitability, accounting and taxes, as well as corporate transformation in line with the strategy defined
for the business, through project guidance and monitoring of high value in both profits and efficiency capture, advanced analytics, data
governance, the development of increasingly digital services and the deployment of the strategy seeking alignment and appropriation by
the employees of our Bank.
## Bank Experience: 34 years
Responsible for advising, directing and coordinating, the aspects in which our Bank and its Affiliates have to do with the national and
international legal system, contributing to the defense of their interests before any body, as well as to the normalization and/or recovery
of the portfolio of companies.
## Business Administrator
## Bank Experience: 4 years
## Previous experience at Grupo Aval: 8 years
Responsible for defining strategic plans that allow the development of lasting and profitable relationships with affluent individuals, originating
in places where demand is generated (Vehicle Dealers and Construction Companies) and deepened through a value proposition of service
and investment, financing and insurance products. In addition, responsible for customer segmentation, value proposition development,
product profitability measurement, business intelligence, customer experience monitoring, customer experience development services and
monitoring of these in the different physical and electronic channels, as well as the definition of strategies for activation, use and billing
through Credit and Debit Cards, as well as the generation of profits by return of commissions, as a result of the placement of insurance
policies that minimize the risk exposure of the Bank and our clients.
Economist and International Negotiator, with a Master’s Degree in Finance.
## Industry experience: 20 years
Responsible for planning and directing policies and strategies related to the Business market, focused on the growth of Assets (placements)
and Liabilities (Deposits) in the Government, Corporate and Business (SMEs) segments.
