## At Banco de Occidente, we reaffirm our commitment
to the well-being of our employees through a robust
## Occupational Health and Safety Management System
(OH&S-MS) aligned with the highest standards. Our
strategy not only responds to regulatory requirements,
but also seeks to generate a positive impact on the
organizational culture, promoting safe, healthy and
sustainable work environments.
## Occupational Health
## At Banco de Occidente, we are committed to the
safety and well-being of our employees, contractors,
and anyone under our supervision. Our Occupational
## Health and Safety policy is applicable to all our
operations and complies with relevant national
and international regulations, as well as applicable
voluntary standards and collective agreements.
## We encourage the consultation and participation
of our employees and their representatives in
the identification of risks, the implementation of
preventive measures and the continuous improvement
of our OSH management system. To this end, we set
quantifiable objectives, prioritize action plans and
promote a culture of prevention in all our areas of
## As part of our commitment to sustainability and
improving organizational performance, we report
our OSH progress within the framework of key
indicators used in assessments such as the Dow
Jones Sustainability Index (DJSI). This allows us to
continuously evaluate and optimize our performance
metrics, ensuring responsible management aligned
with industry best practices.
## Our Executive Management endorses and promotes the
implementation of this policy, ensuring its integration
into our corporate strategy and its alignment with
our values of well-being and sustainability.
## Our Occupational Health
and Safety Program
## At Banco de Occidente, we carry out periodic risk
and hazard assessments in the field of Occupational
## Health and Safety (OSH) to identify and mitigate
factors that may affect the well-being of our
employees and contractors. We prioritize and
integrate action plans with quantifiable objectives,
aligned with our corporate strategy.
## We have emergency prevention and response
protocols, regular internal inspections and
external audits under national and international
standards and rules, ensuring compliance
and continuous improvement of our OSH
management system.
## We investigate any injury, illness, or workplace
incident in a structured manner to implement
corrective actions. In addition, we offer OSH
training to employees and third parties,
promoting a culture of prevention. OSH
criteria are incorporated into our contracting
requirements, ensuring our partners’
commitment to safety.
## Preventive strategy
## During 2024, we strengthened the management of
the OSHMS through a preventive approach based
on risk identification, the implementation of wellness
programs, and the use of technology to optimize
processes. Our management is focused on three
fundamental pillars:
## Ri sk prevention and health promotion: We
implement action plans to mitigate ergonomic,
psychosocial and biological risk factors,
ensuring optimal working conditions for all
## C ulture of safety and well-being: We develop
training and awareness programs aimed at
promoting a culture of self-care and co-
responsibility in safety and health at work.
## M onitoring and continuous improvement:
Through key indicators and internal audits,
we evaluate the performance of the system
to implement improvements aligned with our
sustainability strategy..
## In 2024, we strengthened and expanded the coverage
of the OSHMS, managing to intervene in all our offices
and guaranteeing the inclusion of all employees,
regardless of their work modality. In addition, we
extended the scope of the system to contractors
operating within our facilities and to suppliers with
personnel on mission, reaffirming our commitment
to safety throughout the value chain.
## With these actions, we continue to consolidate a
culture of prevention and well-being, ensuring that
## Banco de Occidente continues to be a benchmark
in the protection of the health and safety of our
employees and allies.
## Actions Implemented
## To strengthen our OSHMS, we have developed a
comprehensive plan that includes the planning
and definition of OSHMS policies, indicators and
objectives, as well as the development of work plans
for the different surveillance and prevention programs.
## These include psychosocial surveillance systems and
prevention of musculoskeletal disorders, industrial
safety and hygiene, occupational medicine and
reintegration processes, emergency preparedness
and response, strategic road safety plan and public
risk management. In addition, we worked on the
pregnant women and breastfeeding program,
the communication and training plan in OSH, the
strengthening of employees participation committees
such as COPASST, the Coexistence Committee and
the Road Safety Committee, and the program for
suppliers and contractors.
## Impact on Stakeholders
## These actions generate positive impacts both on our
employees and on the external actors who interact
with our organization. Thanks to its implementation,
we ensure compliance with current legal regulations
and promote health and prevention of occupational
diseases. We create safe and healthy work
environments, facilitate timely and efficient return
to work, and ensure operational continuity in case
of emergencies. We also contribute to safety in the
mobilization of employees, promote education and
generate safe spaces for breastfeeding. We are also
strengthening employees engagement channels and
extending the reach of OSHMS to key contractors
and suppliers to ensure safety throughout the value
Hazard identification,
risk assessments and
incident investigation
## At our bank, we manage occupational health and
safety through a structured approach based on
hazard identification, risk assessment and incident
investigation. We implement an Occupational Health
and Safety Management System (OSHMS) aligned
with local regulations and international standards,
guaranteeing a safe work environment for our
## To identify hazards, we carry out periodic inspections
at the facilities, surveys of employees and analysis
of working conditions, to detect potential risks.
## We use qualitative and quantitative risk analysis
methodologies to prioritize and mitigate threats.
## In terms of risk assessment, we apply the risk
matrix to classify and manage hazards related to
ergonomic, psychosocial, biological and physical
factors. In addition, we promote operational controls
and specific action plans to minimize exposure to
occupational risks.
## Regarding incident investigation, we follow a
standardized protocol that includes information
gathering, root cause analysis, and establishing
improvement plans. An internal committee analyzes
incidents and accidents at work, ensuring the
application of corrective and preventive measures
to prevent their recurrence.
## In addition, we carry out constant training in
occupational safety, promote a culture of risk
reporting, and work on the continuous improvement
of the OSHMS to guarantee the well-being of our
## In our bank, we have a comprehensive occupational
health program that contributes to the identification,
prevention and minimization of occupational risks,
guaranteeing the well-being of our employees. We
implement a comprehensive rehabilitation program,
through which we monitor and accompany employees
who have health conditions that may generate
prolonged disabilities (greater than 30 days) or that
require restrictions or work recommendations.
## In addition, we carry out periodic occupational
medical examinations of the entire working population,
ensuring early detection of risk factors and timely
intervention. Every year, we carry out an occupational
health diagnosis, analyzing the main findings of
occupational assessments, which allows us to plan
preventive activities and intervention strategies in
the field of occupational medicine.
## To ensure quality and access to these services, we
work with a team of health professionals, including
occupational physicians, physiotherapists, and
occupational psychologists, who provide ongoing
accompaniment. We also maintain strategic alliances
with nationally certified providers for the provision
of specialized medical services.
Employees engagement,
consultation and communication
## At Banco de Occidente, we guarantee the participation
of our employees in the Occupational Health and
## Safety Management System (OSHMS) through
different mechanisms of consultation, communication
and decision-making.
## Joint Committee on
## Hazard Identification
and Risk Assessment
## A space for participation where employees elect
representatives to ensure the continuous improvement
of the OSHMS. Monthly meetings and training on
responsibilities and improvement actions are held.
## Focused on the prevention of traffic accidents and awareness
of road safety regulations through training and work plans
aimed especially at employees in commercial roles.
## Space where employees can report complaints related
to the work environment, with follow-up and resolution
of conflicts in a framework of confidentiality.
## Through COPASST, the OSH mailbox (saludytrabajo@
bancodeoccidente.com.co), safety inspections and
the annual update of risk management, employees
can report conditions and propose improvements.
## It is in charge of socializing and coordinating the
## Emergency Plan, carrying out earthquake and
evacuation drills in all offices nationwide.
## Employees actively participate in safety inspections
and job analysis to identify and mitigate risks.
## Employees training on
## EAt Banco de Occidente, occupational health and
safety (OSH) is a fundamental pillar of our well-
being and occupational risk prevention strategy. To
this end, we have implemented a comprehensive
training program aimed at all our employees and
employees who, although they are not a direct part of
our organization, carry out their work in our facilities
or under our supervision.
## As part of our OSH management, we identify
training needs through a methodology based on
risk assessment and evaluation, where we analyze
the specific hazards of each job. This process
is complemented by the information obtained
through the Epidemiological Surveillance System,
occupational health diagnosis, investigations of
occupational incidents and diseases, as well as
analysis of the current epidemiological context.
## In this way, we ensure that our training is relevant
and responds to the real working conditions of our
## The trainings are designed in conjunction with
specialized professionals, both internal and
external, including experts from our Occupational
## Risk Administrator (ARL), health service provider
institutions (IPS) and strategic allies. These trainings
can be given in face-to-face, virtual or hybrid mode,
depending on the nature of the topics addressed.
## In addition, in some cases, theoretical-practical
sessions are included to ensure a better appropriation
## Among the main trainings we offer is induction
and re-induction in the Occupational Health and
## Safety Management System (OSHMS), aimed at
all the bank’s employees, including providers of
general, temporary, technology, surveillance and
security services. In addition, we have specialized
courses, such as training at heights for employees
who perform work in this type of conditions, training
in the safe handling of chemical substances for
storage and logistics personnel, and the course in
the proper use of personal protective equipment
(PPE) for populations exposed to specific risks.
## Another pillar of our training strategy is the training of
the teams responsible for emergency management.
## On a voluntary basis, all employees can participate
in the emergency brigade course, although for those
who are an active part of these groups, training is
mandatory. This training covers topics such as first
aid, cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) maneuvers,
management of syncope, shock and seizures, care
for metabolic pathologies such as diabetes and
hypertension, fire control and evacuation plans.
## Similarly, the members of the Emergency Operating
## Committee (COE) receive training on roles and
responsibilities in crisis management, as well as in
the identification and response to early warnings in
our facilities nationwide.
## In the field of workplace well-being, we promote
training in ergonomics, active breaks and postural
hygiene, aimed at employees in administrative areas,
## Formal safety and health committees
## Emergency Operating
## Representatives of employees
and employer (8 per zone).
It is carried out monthly.
## Responsible for the Strategic
Road Safety Plan. It is
carried out permanently.
## Leaders of Administrative
## Resources, Human Talent, Banking
Security, OSH and Operations. It
is carried out according to need.
2 main representatives and
2 alternates on behalf of the
employer and employees. It is

## Supervise the OSHMS, propose
improvements and participate
in accident investigations.
## Carry out preventive activities
and analyze road incidents.
## Coordinate prevention and
response actions in emergencies.
Prevent workplace harassment,
manage complaints and
promote coexistence.
to reduce risks associated with prolonged postures
and work fatigue. These sessions are taught both
face-to-face and virtual and are offered periodically
throughout the year. We also carry out training
aimed at mental health and stress management,
such as training in resilience coping strategies for
the members of the Work Coexistence Committee.
## Within the framework of Health Week, we hold training
sessions on healthy lifestyle habits, where topics such
as balanced diet, prevention of chronic diseases and
promotion of physical and emotional well-being are
addressed. In addition, we have developed specific
programs on road safety, with special emphasis
on accident prevention, safe driving, reduction of
distractions at the wheel and protection of vulnerable
## All these trainings are given at no cost to employees
and during working hours, ensuring that participation
does not affect their remuneration. In some cases,
certain training is mandatory, especially those related
to the prevention of critical risks. To evaluate the
effectiveness of our training actions, we implement
satisfaction surveys and knowledge tests, which
allows us to monitor the impact of the programs
and identify opportunities for improvement in the
management of occupational health and safety.
## Through this comprehensive strategy, we reaffirm
our commitment to the protection and well-being of
our employees, promoting an organizational culture
based on prevention and self-care.
## During 2024, we reaffirm our commitment to the
safety and well-being of our employees through a
robust Occupational Health and Safety (OSH) training
program. During the year, 559 training activities
were carried out aimed at the organization’s direct
employees, reaching a cumulative 4,510 hours of
training. Thanks to these initiatives, we were able to
train 7 ,007 people in various OSH topics, ensuring
the strengthening of the culture of prevention in the
## Within these activities, the training with the greatest
coverage impacted 6,790 employees, demonstrating
the importance and scope of our education strategies
in occupational risk prevention.
## In addition, understanding the relevance of safety
for all those who provide services in our facilities,
we also extended our training strategy to suppliers
and contractors. In the same period, we carried out
57 training activities specifically aimed at these
groups, managing to train 541 people with a total
of 29 hours of training.
## With these actions, we seek not only to comply
with regulatory standards, but also to strengthen
an organizational culture focused on prevention
and self-care, impacting both our employees and
the strategic allies that are part of our operations.
## Promotion of the health and
well-being of employees
## At the Bank, we guarantee that our employees have
the necessary time to attend medical appointments
and procedures, regardless of whether they are
scheduled by their EPS, policy or prepaid medical
insurance service. Each leader manages these
permissions and, when necessary, Human Talent
and OSH provide support to ensure compliance.
## In addition, we offer health promotion programs:
## H ealth Week: Annual event with talks and
activities on wellness, disease prevention and
## B lood Donation Day: Spaces in our facilities
where health entities facilitate the collection
## Ex ecutive Check-Up Program:  Specialized
medical exams for senior management, middle
management, and management.
## Nut ritional Challenge: Accompaniment
in healthy habits for overweight or obese
## These initiatives reinforce our commitment to the
health and well-being of our teams.
## Prevention and mitigation of health and
safety risks in business relations
Through our Occupational Health and Safety Management System (OSHMS), we
implement preventive measures based on the hierarchy of controls to mitigate or
eliminate risks identified in our assessments.
The main focus is on the safety of our customers and visitors in bank branches. In the
event of incidents, we carry out investigations to identify causes and apply corrective
or preventive actions to prevent their recurrence.
This commitment allows us to ensure a safe environment in all our operations.
In 2024, we will continue to rigorously monitor our accident, severity, and absenteeism
indicators, to strengthen our prevention and workplace well-being strategies.
403-9 Workplace Injury
## AT Injuries: (Recordable Work Accident Events/HHT) *1,000,000
The rate of occupational accidents stood at 4.1, reflecting an increase compared to the previous year. This increase is directly
related to the greater number of reported events, which in turn may be a consequence of the growth in our workforce, and
therefore a greater exposure to operational risks.
In the case of our contractors, the accident rate remained at zero, which shows adequate safety management in outsourced
activities. In the combined total of employees and contractors, the rate stood at 4.1, which reaffirms the need to continue
strengthening our strategies for the prevention and mitigation of occupational risks.
In terms of occupational diseases, in 2024 two cases were reported in our workforce, which represents a reduction compared
to the previous year. This decrease was related to better identification and management of occupational risk factors, as well
as the strengthening of ergonomics, active breaks and health monitoring.
For contractors, the rate of occupational diseases remained at zero, and in the total (employees and contractors), the number
of cases also decreased compared to 2023. However, we continue to strengthen our occupational health initiatives to minimize
the incidence of work-related diseases and improve the quality of life of our employees.
The accident severity index for our employees stood at 3.80, which represents a
reduction compared to the previous year. This behavior is associated with a decrease
in days of disability due to work accidents, which is related to better risk management
and effective return to work processes.
For our contractors, the severity rate remains at zero, reflecting an adequate management
of safety standards in the execution of their activities. In the combined total of employees
and contractors, the severity index reached 3.78, showing an improvement over the
## These results highlight the positive impact of
our preventive initiatives and the effectiveness
of the safety protocols implemented, which
have allowed us to reduce the impact of
accidents in terms of days of disability.
## ISA Formula: (Workers’ Disability Days/HHT) *100
HHT: 122,176 contractors.
The accident frequency rate stood at 0.81 for employees, which represents an increase compared
to the previous year. This increase is related to a greater number of incidents reported in the
year, which is also linked to a growth in our workforce, and, therefore, to a greater exposure
to occupational risks.
For contractors, the rate remained at zero,  which reaffirms the effectiveness of our safety
strategies in outsourced processes. In the combined total, the frequency index reached 0.81,
prompting us to further strengthen our accident prevention training and awareness initiatives.
## Accident Frequency
The absenteeism rate for employees in 2024 was 14.23, reflecting an increase
compared to the previous year. This increase is mainly associated with a greater
number of days of disability due to common events, which highlights the
need to continue promoting the comprehensive well-being of our employees,
including aspects of physical and mental health.
For contractors, the absenteeism rate reached 76.42, influenced by the increase
in days of disability due to common events. This translates into the importance
of continuing to strengthen the well-being and health conditions for this group,
in line with our occupational health and safety standards.
In the combined total of employees and contractors, the absenteeism rate
stood at 14.30, which motivates us to continue promoting disease prevention
initiatives and the strengthening of healthy habits in the work environment.

## IFA Formula: (Accidents at Work Cases/HHT) *100

is to be a key ally in their development, promoting

offered by the market, we reaffirm our commitment
to financial education, regulatory updating and the

the positive impact of our clients on the economy

## At Banco de Occidente, we understand that the
success of our clients is the engine of economic
and business development. For this reason, we
are constantly working to provide them with tools
and knowledge that allow them to make informed
decisions, optimize their operations and strengthen
their competitiveness in an ever-evolving environment.
## Inclusive assistance
## To strengthen the culture of inclusion and respect, we
provide annual trainings to our staff, ensuring that
they acquire the necessary skills to properly serve
people with disabilities. These trainings include:
## V irtual course on care for people with
disabilities: It provided practical tools for
interaction with people with different types of
disabilities (hearing, visual, physical, among
others), including simulations and guidelines
to offer an inclusive and efficient service.
## Vir tual course on the Financial Consumer
## Service System (SAC): Reinforced knowledge
on the protection of the rights of financial
consumers, with a special focus on the most
vulnerable groups.

## Course: Explained the role of the Ombudsman
and their work in protecting the interests of all

better communication with people with hearing
As a result of our commitment to financial inclusion,
by 2024 we were able to:
## S erve 12 hearing-impaired
customers across our different
## A dapt 173 branches with
improved accessibility.
## T rain 5,910 employees in
inclusive assistance.
## With these initiatives, we
reaffirm our responsibility to
offer an accessible banking
service, ensuring that all people,
regardless of their condition, can
access our financial solutions
in an equitable and dignified
## Banking Correspondents
## Our Correspondent Banking channel has been key
to expanding access to financial services in rural
communities and disadvantaged sectors, where the
presence of bank branches is limited. During 2024,
we managed to increase the number of service points
in hard-to-reach regions, going from 53,000 in 2023
to 90,000 by the end of the year. This facilitated
essential transactions such as bill payments, PILA,
withdrawals, and deposits, as well as boosting the
banking penetration of the Dale! digital wallet in
the correspondent Point of Payment, with more
than 59,000 connections in the last four months of
the year. With these actions, we have reduced the
geographical and economic barriers that exclude
many people from the formal financial system.
## To ensure that our services are accessible and
understandable to everyone, we have worked
on simplifying and standardizing processes at
correspondent banking points. We prioritize the
training of correspondents, who are the first point
of contact with communities, ensuring that they
provide clear and effective guidance. We also design
materials and signage in an accessible language,
making it easy to use our services regardless of the
educational level of the users. Although we have
not yet implemented specific financial education
programs with the communities, we focus on making
our service points work as an extension of the
## Bank, bringing services closer in a practical and
understandable way.
## Although the Banking Correspondent Unit has not
developed specific financial education programs,
at a global level we promote educational initiatives
through communication campaigns. These seek
to raise awareness among customers about the
responsible use of credit and savings, indirectly reaching the communities served
by banking correspondents. In this sense, correspondents play a fundamental role
in making it easier for users to understand how to carry out basic transactions
safely and effectively, reinforcing our commitment to financial inclusion.
We measure the social impact of our banking correspondents through key
indicators such as the increase in Dale!’s banking penetration and transaction
volume. In 2024, we achieved a 14% growth in transactions and  1,987% in
Dale!’s banking penetration, contributing significantly to the financial inclusion
of these communities.
In terms of sustainability, in 2024 we began the implementation of the Stand-in
initiative, designed to ensure the operational continuity of banking correspondents
even in cases of disruptions in our systems. With this system, customers can
make payments and other transactions without being affected by operational
outages, ensuring a smooth and reliable experience. In addition to improving
efficiency, this initiative reduces the need for additional travel for users, reinforcing
our commitment to accessibility and sustainability in financial services.
We also implemented several innovations in our Correspondent Banking channel
to improve its efficiency and sustainability:
Migr ation of the dedicated SuperGIROS and Point of Payment channel,
which reduced operational interruptions and improved the customer
P hase 2 of SuperGIROS, which enabled new services such as cardless
withdrawals from the Aval Banks and Dale!, as well as deposits and
payments to third parties in cash.
Incr ease in ceilings in Efecty, going from $500,000 to $1,000,000 per
collection, facilitating transactions of greater amounts.
N ew strategic alliances with networks such as Farmatodo and Gane
through Puntored, expanding our coverage and bringing our financial
services even closer to customers.
## With these advances, we continue to strengthen our commitment
to financial inclusion and accessibility, ensuring that more
and more people have access to quality banking services.

## Correspondent Points 2023 2024 Change (%)
## CX Awards: Recognizing
excellence in customer
## At Banco de Occidente, we are committed to offering
an exceptional experience to our customers. As part of
this commitment, every year we hold the CX Awards,
an event in which we recognize the areas, offices
and employees that have implemented successful
initiatives and have generated a positive impact on
## The selection of the awarded projects is made through
a call open to all areas of the bank. During this
phase, the areas nominate the projects implemented
throughout the year that have significantly improved
the customer experience. A specialized team evaluates
each initiative and defines the finalists. Subsequently,
an evaluation jury analyzes the projects and selects
the winners based on predefined criteria. Winners are
announced and recognized at the annual customer
## Categories evaluated
## The CX Awards include six categories, each
highlighting a key aspect in the transformation of
the customer experience:
## B est Projects: We recognize teams that
have implemented innovative solutions and
transformed key moments in our customers’
journey. These projects have used customer
experience tools to generate tangible impacts
on the bank’s profitability and customer
## B est ENSI (Internal Satisfaction Level): We
celebrate areas that have achieved maximum
internal customer satisfaction. This recognition
highlights the synergy between areas, teamwork,
accessibility and empathy in service.
## W OW Offices: We recognize offices that
have excelled in exceptional customer service
performance. The evaluation considers
satisfaction studies, unknown customer results,
participation in customer experience campaigns,
and implementation of improvement plans.
## W OW Employees: We recognize those
employees who have exceeded expectations,
creating unforgettable experiences for our
customers. These employees have been praised
by customers and internal teams, leaving an
indelible mark on every interaction.
## B est Managers: We distinguish commercial
managers with the highest levels of
recommendation from our clients. Their advice,
solutions, accompaniment and empathy have
made them benchmarks within the bank.
## Exper ience Ambassadors: A group of employees
from different areas who inspire the organization
to achieve memorable experiences. They
participate in the co-creation of solutions to
challenges identified in the customer experience
and promote best practices in their day-to-day
## Tangible impact on customer experience
During 2024, we received more than 30 projects at the CX Awards. Most of these
were successfully implemented, generating positive results both in terms of revenue
and customer experience.
## The distribution of awards in 2024 was as follows:
B est Projects: 3 winners.
## B est ENSI: 14 recognized areas
W OW Offices:  17 featured offices.
## W OW Employees:  14 award-
winning employees.
B est Managers: 30 managers recognized.
## Exper ience Ambassadors:  5
distinguished employees.
## Through the CX Awards, we reaffirm our commitment
to innovation, continuous improvement and excellence
in customer service. We will continue to promote
initiatives that strengthen the customer experience
and recognize those who, with their dedication and
effort, contribute to making Banco de Occidente
## Customer Recommendation
## At Banco de Occidente, customer experience is
a strategic priority. Our overall NPS has remained
stable in recent years, reaching 68% in 2024, close
to our goal of 70%. In Business Banking, we achieved
an increase of 2 percentage points, reaching the
best NPS in the last four years and reflecting an
improvement in the perception of our business
customers. In Personal Banking, the indicator
remained stable compared to 2023, although we
continue to work on opportunities for improvement
to strengthen our customers’ recommendation and
achieve the proposed goals.
To better understand the perception of our customers,
we carry out constant measurements that allow us to
identify strengths and areas of opportunity. We look
at what our customers value most and the critical
aspects we need to focus on to reduce friction in
the experience. Our promoter clients highlight the
quality of our service and the portfolio of products and services, key factors in loyalty. On the other hand,
passive and detractor customers show a lower recommendation when they perceive that we are not timely
and close enough in service, or when they encounter difficulties with our products, services and channels.
In 2024, we evaluated the satisfaction of 4,584 customers, representing 0.46% of our customer base, with
samples selected under statistical criteria that guarantee representativeness and a margin of error equal
to or less than 5%. This methodology, endorsed by IZO, an international expert provider of quantitative
measurements, ensures the reliability of our results.
Specifically, we attended to  252,160 requests,
resolving 250,849 of them (99.48%), and managed
24,560 complaints, of which 24,492 were resolved
These results reflect our commitment to transparency,
continuous improvement and customer satisfaction.
## Customer Experience
## Excellence Training
## At Banco de Occidente, we believe that an exceptional
experience for our customers starts with highly
trained teams aligned with our service culture. For this
reason, we promote training programs designed to
strengthen the skills and knowledge of our employees
in each business segment, ensuring that they can
provide expert advice, personalized solutions and
attention that generates trust and loyalty.
## Throughout the year, we have developed training
initiatives focused on each of our banks and segments,
addressing key topics such as understanding
customer needs, process optimization, digitalization
and service excellence. These programs have allowed
us to improve interaction with our customers and
enhance the recommendation of our products and
measurement 2021 2022 2023 2024 What was the goal
for the year 2024?
While some goals were not fully achieved, the stability in our metrics and growth in key segments demonstrate
the positive impact of our strategies on the customer experience. We continue to strengthen our actions to
ensure that our measurements accurately reflect the perception of our customers, mitigating any impact on
our external assessments such as the DJSI.
Mechanisms for Petitions, Complaints,
## Claims and Suggestions – PQRS
At Banco de Occidente, we manage our clients’ Requests, Complaints, Claims, and Suggestions (PQRS) with
commitment and efficiency. During 2024, we received a total of 276,720 requests,  of which 275,341 were
resolved, reaching a 99.50% effectiveness in the response.
## Corporate banking training
## At Banco de Occidente, we understand the
importance of keeping our clients informed and
updated on the main challenges and opportunities of
the economic, financial and regulatory environment.
## For this reason, we develop training spaces aimed
at entrepreneurs and strategic customers, with the
aim of strengthening their decision-making capacity,
optimizing risk management and promoting the
sustainable growth of their businesses.
## Over the last year, we have carried out several
training sessions on key topics for the business
sector, addressing everything from macroeconomic
challenges to investment opportunities in renewable
energy. Below are some of the most notable events:
## E valuation of the main challenges for the
## Colombian entrepreneur: 2-hour session in
which the global and local challenges that
affect the country’s economic growth were
analyzed, with emphasis on macroeconomic
risks and political and fiscal uncertainty. 45
businessmen participated, with an investment
of $2,824,640.
## B ogotá Treasury Forum: 2-hour meeting where
the challenges of the local and international
economy in terms of growth, inflation,
exchange rate and monetary policy were
addressed, providing key tools for strategic
decision-making. 40 customers attended, with
an investment of $2,140,560.
## P hotovoltaic Energy Forum: Space of 2 hours
and 30 minutes in which the different tax
incentives available for investments in solar
power projects were presented, highlighting
their environmental impact and contribution to
sustainable development. It had the participation
## T alk on Exchange Regime: Session of 1 hour
and 30 minutes in which clients were updated
on the exchange regulation of the DIAN,
providing key information to optimize foreign
trade transactions. 75 customers participated.
## O cciRed Forum: Update and learn more about
## OcciRed: 2-hour space where the news of
the OcciRed portal was presented, along with
security recommendations to protect customers’
financial operations. It had 10 customers.
## These spaces have been highly valued by our
clients, as they allow them to analyze in greater depth
the economic, regulatory and technological context
in which they operate, improving their management
and decision-making capacity. In addition, they
reinforce our loyalty strategy, strengthening the
bank’s commercial relationship and closeness to
## Government banking training
## As part of our commitment to the efficient management
of public finances and institutional strengthening,
we have developed multiple training spaces for
government entities at the territorial and national
levels. These initiatives have made it possible to
update and strengthen the capacities of employees
in tax regulations, financial management and key
digital tools for public administration.
## Here are some of the most outstanding trainings:
## S tamps - Tax Regulations: 2-hour training
aimed at the Government of Cauca, in which
the current regulations on Tax Stamps were
deepened. We had 75 attendees and an
investment of $1,081,428.
## C hange Management:  Training space for the
municipality of Yumbo, focused on strategies
for the implementation of CS3 software,
with the aim of optimizing the process of
adopting organizational change. 60 employees
participated, with an investment of $980,000.
## S ECOP II:  2-hour training aimed at the
municipality of Medina, with the purpose of
strengthening the knowledge and applicability
of the SECOP II platform in future public
procurement. It had 58 attendees and an
investment of $754,000.
## D evelopment Plan: Advice for the municipality
of Puerto Tejada in the construction of its
## Development Plan, providing tools for its
alignment with the current regulatory framework.
35 employees participated and the investment
was $754,000.
In addition, we have developed other key trainings,
## M anagement of resources of the General System
of Participations (SGP) for the municipality of
## T erritorial taxes and property tax for the District
## F inancial diagnosis and budgetary control for
the Department of Huila and the Municipality
## S ettlement and collection of property tax for
the Department of Nariño.
## Mult ipurpose Cadastre for the Municipality of
## Di ssaving in Pension Liabilities - FONPET, aimed
at multiple municipalities, including La Vega
(Cauca), Antioquia, Santa Fe de Antioquia,
## Ebéjico, Soata, Pailitas (Cesar), Coveñas, among
## In total, these trainings have represented an investment
of $24,685,423, impacting numerous municipalities
and departments of the country. Through these
spaces, we have been able to strengthen public
management, improve fiscal planning, and support
compliance with key regulations in the government
growth and strengthening of our business customers
and SMEs. Through our training, we provide key tools
to improve the competitiveness and sustainability of
their businesses, facilitating updating on strategic
and operational issues.

trainings, designed to meet the needs of our clients
in areas such as digital transformation, leadership,
human talent management, labor law, sustainability,
artificial intelligence and strategic planning.

## With Maestros del Hacer, we reaffirm our
commitment to sustainable development,
promoting financial inclusion and economic
empowerment of the communities in which
At Banco de Occidente, we believe in the transformative power of knowledge and
education as fundamental pillars for the development of communities. Our commitment
goes beyond financial solutions, encompassing initiatives that promote inclusive growth,
sustainability and the generation of opportunities for different sectors of society.
Through training spaces, we strengthen the social and business fabric of communities,
providing practical tools that allow them to improve their quality of life, enhance their
skills and access new economic opportunities. Our focus is on capacity building,
empowering vulnerable groups, and fostering sustainable practices that contribute
to a more equitable and resilient future.
At the bank, we reaffirm our purpose of generating a positive impact on the communities
with which we interact, consolidating ourselves as a key player in the promotion of
social well-being and sustainable growth.
## Strengthening access to water
and sanitation in vulnerable
communities with UNICEF
## At Banco de Occidente, we are committed to the social and
economic development of the country’s most vulnerable
communities. In line with this purpose, for three years we have
strengthened our alliance with UNICEF and the WASH (Water,
## Sanitation and Hygiene) program, through which we promote
a donation program that has allowed us to generate a real
impact in La Guajira. Thanks to this joint effort, we have brought
drinking water and better sanitation conditions to more than
26 communities in the municipalities of Manaure and Uribia,
transforming their environment and quality of life.
UNICEF’s work is critical to ensuring children’s rights in more
than 190 countries and territories, and in Colombia, its focus on
water, sanitation and hygiene has led to solutions tailored to the
needs of the most vulnerable communities. Through the WASH
program, we work so that children and adolescents have access
to drinking water and can grow up in healthy environments that
promote their well-being and integral development.
## Our support has been key to the construction of six new water
supply systems, contributing to sustainable access to basic
sanitation services. In 2024, we allocated $283,703,267 to this
program with the valuable contribution of 1,122 customers, and
since 2022, we have donated a total of $1,910,229,925. Thanks
to this joint effort, we have been able to:
## Gener ate impact in 1,028 people, including 343 children
and adolescents, in 24 communities.
## P rovide access for 4,100 people to basic sanitation, of
which 1,000 live in emergency contexts.
C ertify 1,500 people as free of open defecation.
12, 000 people in humanitarian emergency contexts with
access to safe drinking water and quality services.
14  strengthened health centers with access to water,
sanitation and hygiene, promoting the right to health of
children and adolescents.
20 ,600 menstruating girls and adolescents benefited from
menstrual hygiene supplies and education, of which 20,000
live in humanitarian emergency contexts.
This impact transcends access to basic services,
since having drinking water and adequate sanitation
allows other fundamental rights, such as health,
nutrition, education and child protection, to be
guaranteed. Improving conditions in schools and
communities not only prevents diseases and reduces
school dropouts but also promotes a safer and more
dignified environment for the development of children
to the well-being of communities and will continue
to work together with UNICEF to expand our impact,
contributing to the construction of a more sustainable
and equitable future.
## Environmental volunteering
to sustainability and social well-being through
various volunteer initiatives, in which our employees
actively participate to generate a positive impact on
the community and the environment. These activities
allow us to strengthen ties with society, consolidate
our organizational culture and contribute to the
personal and professional development of those
who are part of our team.
## Our achievements in 2024
During the last year, we carried out 14 volunteer days, mobilizing approximately 735 volunteers in
different cities of the country. Among our main achievements are:
1, 943 seedlings planted in strategic natural areas.
4  days in Bogotá, with a constant participation of 60 volunteers per day and a total of 560
seedlings planted.
3  days in Medellín, with the maintenance of trails and reseeding in the Arví Park, with the participation
of 131 volunteers.
3 days in Barranquilla, where 105 volunteers planted 600 seedlings on the Vía Parque Isla Salamanca.
4 day s in Cali, reaching the largest number of seedlings planted (783) and the largest call of the
year with 93 volunteers in September.
2 d ays of social volunteering, impacting the Casita de Belén Foundation in Cali, with the participation
of 36 children and employees, through recreational activities.
Through these actions, we reaffirm our commitment to sustainable
development, integrating our employees and the community in
initiatives that generate positive and lasting impacts.
## Our volunteer days bring us closer to the communities
and strengthen our relationship with them. Through
environmental conservation and social support
activities, we promote sustainable development
and the regeneration of natural spaces, in addition
to contributing to the well-being of vulnerable
populations. This commitment has allowed us
to consolidate ourselves as a responsible and
sustainable entity, generating trust and recognition
in our environment.
## Volunteering gives our employees the opportunity to
strengthen skills such as teamwork, leadership, and
communication, while fostering a strong sense of
social responsibility. These experiences generate a
positive impact on their motivation and commitment,
promoting a greater sense of belonging and pride
in contributing to collective well-being.
## Donations for Social
## At Banco de Occidente, our donations are part
of our Social Investment policy, understood as a
voluntary act through which we provide financial
or in-kind resources with the aim of generating a
positive impact on communities and populations in
vulnerable situations. These donations are part of our
three strategic pillars: environmental conservation,
education for development and conscious social
## Through this approach, we seek to strengthen
initiatives that promote respect for fundamental rights,
contribute to the improvement of people’s quality of
life and foster the sustainable development of the
regions in which we have a presence. Each donation
responds to our commitment to generate value in
society and support the progress of communities
through access to opportunities that allow their
well-being and growth.
## Our donations are aligned with our corporate purpose
of making the development of people, the growth of
businesses and the transformation of the country a
reality. Through these contributions, we expand our
impact to territories and populations that we do
not necessarily reach with our financial operation,
allowing the consolidation of relationships of trust
with communities and social organizations.
## The identification of needs in the territories where
we operate facilitates the establishment of strategic
alliances and the definition of initiatives that
contribute to improving the living conditions of the
population, promoting long-term solutions and
generating shared value with the different actors
## Donations made and
areas of impact in 2024
## During 2024, our donations were aimed at meeting
priority needs in education, economic development
and social welfare. Based on our Social Investment
policy, we carry out actions aimed at strengthening
access to education for children and young people
in vulnerable conditions, guaranteeing opportunities
for complementary training, development of socio-
emotional competencies and skills, as well as support
in their personal and professional growth process.
## In the field of economic development, we promoted
the promotion of local economies through the
identification, articulation and management of
initiatives that promote environmental sustainability
and social progress in different regions of the country.
## These actions allowed us to consolidate alliances
with strategic entities and actors that work to promote
entrepreneurship, employability and capacity building
in communities that require opportunities to improve
their living conditions.
## We also carry out efforts for the implementation
of food security programs and transformation of
life projects, providing beneficiaries with tools for
access to food, income generation and development
of leadership skills. These initiatives contributed to
the construction of more resilient environments and
the generation of opportunities for the growth and
well-being of communities.

## Development of the Pacific

management of a regional agenda that promotes
access to education, the strengthening of the
health system, research in science, and the social
development of vulnerable communities. We benefit
more than 5,165,000 people in Cali and Valle del
## Cauca, prioritizing populations in situations of
exclusion, children and young people, people with
disabilities, ethnic communities and rural population.

## With this donation, we continue to contribute to
the well-being and sustainable development of the
communities that need it most.
## In addition, for this same foundation we allocated
$311,000,000  to strengthen key programs of
## Compromiso Valle, which positively impact various
communities in the region, promoting well-being,

## As part of our commitment to inclusion and social
development, we allocated $7 ,000,000 COP to this
association, benefiting 16 young university students
with the Administrative Process for the Restoration
of Rights at the ICBF. This contribution seeks to
strengthen skills, life projects and the maintenance
of young people who did not have the opportunity
to grow up in a stable and protective family
environment. Many of them have faced situations of
abuse, abandonment and violence, so this initiative
represents a key opportunity for their well-being and
development. The impact of this donation translates
R ecreational, cultural and sports activities,
essential for their emotional well-being, social
integration and consolidation of support
## Imp rovements in the university house, including
rental arrangements and the purchase of a
microwave oven, creating a more welcoming
and functional environment for young people
to concentrate on their studies and life projects.
## With this contribution, we reaffirm our commitment
to provide real opportunities to those who need it
most, promoting their personal and academic growth
in a safe and appropriate environment.
## ProBarranquilla Foundation
## Under our pillar of social growth, we contribute
to the realization of socioeconomic development
projects, promoting the attraction and retention of
investments, business tourism and the management
of the business framework, through the allocation
of $17,833,000.
## With a direct impact on 200 people and more
than 2,000,000 indirect beneficiaries, these actions
generated employment, allowing more people
to access formal job opportunities to improve
their quality of life and guarantee their economic
stability. Additionally, we promoted the reduction of
inequalities by supporting vulnerable communities,
encouraging academic training for human talent;
practices that strengthen infrastructure, drive
sustainable decisions, and foster strategic alliances
for the region’s economic growth.
## We impacted more than 50 elderly people in
vulnerable sectors of Eastern Cali, where we
contributed to improving the quality of life of
the beneficiaries through artistic practices,
psychosocial workshops and productive training, contributing to the strengthening of their livelihoods
and the preservation of their cultural identity. In addition to training in dance, music and plastic arts, the
project included the creation of productive units, promoting income generation and the empowerment of the
participants, our contribution was for $20,000,000.
## Valle del Cauca Action Unit
We impacted 193 people, including social, community and business leaders, through workshops and multi-
stakeholder meetings with the aim of strengthening citizen leadership and transparency in public management.
In addition, 146 young people participated in training on social control, ethics and state contracting, 17
leaders in deepening exercises and 30 businessmen in a dialogue on public credit. These actions promoted
the democratization of information and citizen involvement in decision-making and we also managed to
contribute $48,900,000.
As part of our commitment to social investment, we supported initiatives that have an impact on children and
adolescents in vulnerable situations. Through our monetary contribution of $20,000,000, we also benefited
P oblación Dejando Huellas:  140 girls and boys between 5 and 12 years old, along with 122 families,
strengthened their socio-emotional and cognitive skills through ecology activities, robotics and workshops
on managing emotions. These tools promoted their autonomy, critical thinking and ability to relate
positively to their environment. (Indirect impact: 1,048 people).
P oblación Tejiendo Sueños: 352 school students participated in educational sessions aimed at leadership
development and socio-emotional competencies, indirectly impacting 1,600 people, including teachers,
students and families.
## In addition, on World HIV/AIDS Day, we contributed
to the awareness campaign “Cookie Day”, where
3,000 people received information on HIV/AIDS
prevention and awareness in various shopping malls.
## As part of our customer relationship strategy, in
## December 2024, from the Customer Development
team of the Marketing and Segment Management,
we made an alliance with Fundación Dar Amor,
through which we delivered notebooks with messages
alluding to the purpose of the foundation to different
customers. In addition, we made a social contribution
of $8,000,000 COP to the “School of Peace”
program, which promotes sustainable pedagogical
development and environmental conservation in
rural areas of northern Cauca. This program uses
educational materials and activities designed for the
holistic growth of children and adolescents with HIV.
## This initiative allowed us to transform a relationship
activity into an action with social impact, aligned with
our social investment model and our commitment to
the development of the most vulnerable communities.
## Government Banking and
its impact on society
## At Government Banking, we reaffirm our commitment
to social development through initiatives aimed at
vulnerable populations, especially minors, indigenous
and ethnic communities. Our purpose is to contribute
to the well-being of these communities through the
delivery of school kits, Christmas toys, items for
people with disabilities, among other essential
elements that strengthen social programs focused
on improving the quality of life and the integral
development of people.
All these actions are part of a voluntary contribution,
mainly aimed at social strengthening, without
neglecting the environmental and economic impact
on the public entities with which we work.
## As part of our focus on social equity and respect for
the human conditions of survival, we have promoted
various initiatives in rural sectors and surrounding
communities, coordinating efforts with government
entities to bring aid at different times of the year:
## D elivery of toys in December: Benefit for
children in vulnerable situations, promoting
inclusion and access to recreational spaces.
## S chool kits: Provision of school supplies to
facilitate access to education in communities
with economic constraints.
## F armer’s kits: Provision of basic tools for
agricultural work, supporting productivity in
rural communities.
## F ood and health aid: Assistance to communities
affected by forced displacement emergencies,
guaranteeing basic supplies for their well-being.
## A rticles for people with disabilities:  Provision
of implements that improve the quality of life
and promote inclusion.
## The investments for these initiatives vary according
to the program and the needs of the beneficiary
community, with contributions ranging from $500,000
to $8,000,000 COP per initiative.
## Within the framework of our sustainability strategy, and
aligned with the axis of social growth, we successfully
completed our initiative, where we managed to bring
joy to 6,519 children in vulnerable situations, aged
between 0 and 12 years, in different regions of the
country. The delivery of toys represented a total
contribution of $244,330,000, distributed as follows:

measure the scope of each initiative through the
total population benefited by each contribution,
allowing us to evaluate and optimize our social
intervention strategies. Highlighting that all the
initiatives developed comply with ABAC policies
and are aligned with our strategic axis of social
growth, ensuring that our actions are sustainable,
ethical and with a positive impact on society.

At Banco de Occidente, we are convinced that sustainability is a
collective effort and that true impact is built with the participation
of everyone. This report is a reflection of the commitment of those
who, from different fronts, have contributed to strengthening our
vision of responsible and sustainable business.
To our employees, thank you for your dedication and for making sustainability an essential part
of our day-to-day work. To our customers and allies, for trusting us and motivating us to continue
innovating in solutions that generate shared value. To our communities, for allowing us to be part
of their growth and development, and to our investors and other stakeholders, for accompanying
us on this path and reaffirming with us the importance of integrating environmental, social and
governance criteria in our management.
From the Sustainability Management, we reaffirm our commitment to continue working on the side
of those who do, moving forward with determination in the construction of a more sustainable,
equitable and prosperous future for all. Let’s continue building together.

## Material Issue Omission
19 GRI 2-1 Organizational Details
At Banco de Occidente we are a private financial institution. No government entity or organization
related to state activities owns an equity interest in our company. Our main shareholder is Grupo
Aval Acciones y Valores S.A., which as of December 31, 2023 had a 72.27% stake.
## Ethic al governance
## L eadership committed
## GRI 2-2 Entities included in the
Sustainability reporting.
The information reported focuses on Banco de Occidente S.A. Although we mention our affiliates (FiduOccidente, Banco
de Occidente de Panamá, Occidental Bank Barbados and Nexa BPO) in business performance, the specific sustainability
data does not include these entities. Currently, we have not defined the limits and coverage of environmental and social
indicators, including specific climate indicators in our affiliates.  We are in the process of evaluating how to include this
information in future reports to ensure greater clarity and transparency in the scope of our sustainability reporting.

and point of contact.

19 GRI 2-7 Total contributors by
Of our 6,695 employees, 6,197 have an indefinite term contract and 498 have a fixed-term contract. In addition, we
have 325 trainees and interns, for a total of 7,020 employees, all full-time. In addition, the total number of temporary and
outsourced employees amounts to 2,224, and we do not register personnel under a non-guaranteed hours scheme.

64 GRI 201-1 Direct Economic Value
## Generated and Distributed
Chapter on Responsible Growth and Comprehensive Risk Management; Financial outlook and stock market performance.
Change in the ROI Methodology: to ensure compliance with the requirements of the Dow Jones
Sustainability Index (DJSI), we made an update to the formula used to calculate the Return on
Investment in Human Capital (HC-ROI). In this new methodology, we make sure to:
Total income: includes the total income received by the company before making any
deductions, including interest and valuation income, income from commissions and fees, net
income from financial assets or liabilities held for trading, and other net income.
Total operating expenses: are reported in line with accepted accounting and financial reporting standards,
including general administrative expenses, depreciation and amortization expenses, personnel expenses,
interest and similar expenses, commission and fee expenses, and other operating expenses.
Total employees-related expenses: Considers all labor costs, including salaries,
benefits, training programs, pensions, and severance payments.
Given this adjustment in the methodology, the historical data have been recalculated, which
has generated changes in the previously reported values. HC ROI result recalculated for the last
It is observed that in 2020 there was a significantly high value compared to the following years. This was related to
extraordinary factors, such as financial optimization strategies, impacts derived from the pandemic and/or structural
changes in the bank. From 2021 onwards, the HC ROI stabilizes in a more consistent range, with a decline in 2022 (3.43)
and a gradual recovery in subsequent years (3.65 in 2023 and 3.91 in 2024). This behavior demonstrates a positive
trend in the efficiency of the use of human capital, which we highlight for improvements in productivity, optimization
of labor costs and the strengthening of human talent strategies. This analysis allows us to have a clearer view of the
impact of human capital on the bank’s profitability, ensuring alignment with international sustainability standards.

for democratic processes and the need to maintain a clear separation between politics and business.

79 GRI 207-4 Country-by-
In our 2024 financial statements, we detail income tax following the guidelines of IAS 12 and IAS
1. However, we also assume other taxes and mandatory contributions for our operation, which
are consolidated in different areas. These include the industry and commerce tax, the GMF, the
contribution to the Finance Superintendence, parafiscal contributions, among others.
FN-CB-230a.1 (1) Number of
data breaches, (2) percentage
involving personally identifiable
information (PII), (3) number
of affected account holders
In 2024 we had an incident related to the violation of data privacy, in this case, a complaint was received
from a customer who found on the Scribd platform a document linked to the Bank that contained 119
records, of which 29 corresponded to active customers, after receiving the alert, an investigation was
carried out by the security area,  and there was no evidence of fraud linked to these clients, the document
was removed from the platform and a complaint was filed with the Attorney General's Office against the
former official involved, who worked in one of our affiliates, this incident was handled in accordance with our
security protocols, minimizing the impact and guaranteeing the protection of our clients' information.
Inf ormation security,
system availability
FN-CB-230a.2 Description of
the approach to identifying and
addressing data security risks
At the Bank, we identify and prioritize critical services through the Business Impact Analysis (BIA) exercise,
which allows us to manage the risks associated with the processes and technologies involved, developing
action plans that seek to reduce residual risk and achieve the acceptable level of risk, we have an information
security and cybersecurity governance model,  supported by policies and procedures, such as the Homogeneous
Information Protection Standard (#AVBO. NOSI. SI.005), and we ensure compliance through tools such as DLP
(Data Loss Prevention), IAM (Identity & Access Management) and PAM (Privileged Access Management).
FN-CB-510a.1 Total amount of
monetary losses resulting from
court proceedings related to
fraud, insider trading, antitrust,
unfair competition, market
manipulation, malpractice
or other related financial
industry laws or regulations
During 2024 we had 43 cases related to fraud against customers (electronic fraud that affected
account deposits and TC, consumption not recognized with TC and payment of checks with a forged
signature), in which we were convicted or entered into conciliatory agreements for $387,707,538.

136 GRI 305-5 GHG Emission Reductions
Restatement: We establish the year 2022 as our issuance baseline at Banco de Occidente, due to the stability and
verification of the data obtained for that period. For the years 2022 and 2023, we have carried out a Re-expression of the
information presented in our sustainable management report since the measurement of our carbon footprint was verified
and audited by ICONTEC, which led us to a recalculation of the emissions generated in Scope 1 and 3. For this reason,
the information is subject to a recalculation that considers data from the IPCC's AR6 supplementary material, related
to the confirmation of the Global Warming Potential for Scope 1 (refrigerants). In addition, Resolution 40447 of 2022 of
the Ministry of Mines and Energy updates the fuel blending percentages in motor gasoline and anhydrous ethanol from
90% to 96%, which modifies the Scope 1 (fuels) and Scope 3 (outsourced transportation) information. This recalculation
increased our emissions of:
Total emissions: a total of 9,039.84 emissions in 2022 and 7,395.29 emissions in 2023.

## BdO-9 Workforce Benefits

179 GRI 403-1 Occupational Health and
Safety Management System Chapter Empowering People and Communities; Occupational Health and Safety.
403-2 Hazard Identification,
## Risk Assessment, and
At Banco de Occidente, we carry out a detailed analysis to identify the risks present in all areas of the
organization, including occupational health and safety issues and environmental care. This process allows
us to recognize potential threats and apply proactive measures to reduce their impact. Next, we carry out
a comprehensive risk assessment, where we examine both the probability of occurrence and the possible
consequences of each identified risk. With this information, we set priorities and design specific strategies
to manage them effectively. In the event of an incident, we conduct a thorough investigation to identify the
root causes and implement improvements that optimize our processes and procedures. We focus on avoiding
the repetition of similar events and continuously strengthening our culture of safety and sustainability.
179 GRI 403-3 Occupational
Health Services Chapter Empowering People and Communities; Occupational Health and Safety.
Engagement, Consultation,
and Communication on
At Banco de Occidente, we are constantly striving to promote safe and healthy practices. The safety and health
of all employees, regardless of their type of employment relationship with the organization, is a priority for us.
To ensure the uniform application of these principles throughout our value chain, we have developed robust
monitoring and evaluation mechanisms. Our employees also actively participate in the Occupational Health
and Safety Management System (OH&S-MS). In Colombia, we have the COPASST (Joint Committee on Safety
and Health at Work). This committee is formed in accordance with local regulations, guaranteeing the equitable
representation of employer and employees, and meets monthly to address occupational safety issues, organize
training, carry out safety inspections and lead prevention campaigns. Likewise, we continuously monitor key health
and safety indicators, promoting a constant improvement of working conditions throughout our operation.
179 GRI 403-5 Occupational Health and
Safety Training of Employees Chapter Empowering People and Communities; Occupational Health and Safety.
179 GRI 403-6 Employees
From the Occupational Medicine Program, we have implemented a series of initiatives designed to
promote the comprehensive well-being of our employees, focusing on both physical and mental health
care. These activities seek to generate healthy habits that contribute to a more balanced life, thus
improving their quality of life and work performance. Every year we carry out Health Week, an event
that includes a series of activities focused on the promotion of healthy habits. Activities include:
• Nutrition and healthy eating: workshops and advice on balanced eating.
• Promotion of physical activity: group classes and recommendations for an active life.
• Emotional well-being: talks and workshops on stress management, resilience and mental health.
• Disease prevention: vaccination days and medical check-ups.
• Trends in safety and occupational risks: training in ergonomics and risk prevention.
This event takes place at our administrative headquarters nationwide and is complemented
by virtual activities to ensure the participation of employees in regional offices.
Recreational and cultural activities: throughout the year, we promote physical activity and emotional well-being through
various recreational and cultural activities, coordinated by our work environment team. Some of these include:
• Sports tournaments: beach volleyball competitions and corporate marathons.
• Cultural and social activities: ecological walks, film screenings and volunteer
days to involve employees in social and environmental actions.
In addition, we have established agreements with various sports institutions, such as gyms
and wellness centers, to offer our employees access to facilities and programs that allow
them to maintain a regular exercise routine and improve their quality of life.
403-8 Coverage of the
occupational health and
safety management system
Our OSHMS covers all employees, regardless of their work modality, guaranteeing its application in
all locations. In addition, it includes suppliers and contractors who carry out activities on mission or
within our facilities, ensuring a safe environment aligned with our risk prevention policies.

Climate change represents one of the most significant challenges of our time, and at Banco de Occidente
we are committed to playing an active role in mitigating it. This report, prepared in accordance with the
recommendations of the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD), reflects our dedication to
transparency and climate-related risk management. In this sense, we have developed a sustainability strategy
that seeks to impact society, protect the environment and promote social development in the territories in
The TCFD Initiative is a framework of recommendations designed to promote the disclosure of climate-related
financial information. It seeks to offer solid guidelines that allow companies to communicate more effectively
the financial risks associated with climate change.
In 2022, we began our roadmap for the adoption of the TCFD framework, also aligning with the instructions
of the External Circular No. 031 of 2021 of the Finance Superintendence of Colombia (SFC). This process has
involved different levels and areas of Banco de Occidente, allowing us to address each of the four pillars
and eleven recommendations of the TCFD framework.
As a financial institution committed to the fight against climate change and in compliance with Colombian
legislation, we have adhered to this framework, preparing our first TCFD report in 2023. This adhesion
reinforces our commitment to transparency and management of climate-related financial risks, aligning us
with international best practices.
At Banco de Occidente, we understand that the fight against climate change requires a collective and
continuous effort. We are committed to continuing to work to promote sustainable practices and protect
our planet for future generations.
## Recommended Disclosure TCFD Key Aspect Highlights

structure around climate-related
risks and opportunities.

from the Board of Directors, the Presidency and the different Committees.

• Financial performance.

climate-related scenarios, including a scenario of 2°C or below.
For the prioritization and assessment of climate-related financial risks,
we developed an analysis based on different selection criteria of three
(3) climate scenarios to 2050 based on the NFGS scenarios.

The Bank currently has sustainable lines designed to finance climate change adaptation
and mitigation measures, the balance of the portfolio, the number of loans and the
categories of the Sustainable and Transition Taxonomy defined by the Bank will be
measured to know the impact of these on the environment and the community.
Disclose Scope 1, Scope 2 and, where applicable, Scope 3 GHG emissions and related risks. Since 2022 we have been measuring our scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions, and from 2023 we have
contributed to the creation of a tool that allows us to measure and manage financed emissions.
Describe the objectives used by the Organization to manage climate-related
risks and opportunities and performance against the objectives.
We have defined a goal of 8 trillion by 2030 in sustainable portfolio aligned with
the categories defined in the Bank’s Sustainable and Transition Taxonomy.
In relation to our financed emissions, we started our measurement
with 3 sectors (Energy, Construction and Oil and Gas).
## Alignment with the Sustainability Strategy
With a strong commitment to inclusive economic growth, we work to ensure that our services contribute to
sustainable development, widespread prosperity and resource conservation, ensuring a positive legacy for
future generations.
In this line, we adopt principles of Responsible Business Conduct, integrating and addressing environmental
and social challenges in all our business activities, including our supply chain. Our sustainability strategy is
based on the principles of the Global Compact and the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), as well
as on the adoption of the good practices of the Asobancaria Green Protocol. At Banco de Occidente, we are
dedicated to fostering the development of people, the growth of businesses, and the positive transformation
of the country through innovative financial solutions.

## Alignment with the SDGs
With a commitment to supporting inclusive economic growth, at Banco de Occidente we work to ensure that
our services contribute to sustainable development, extended prosperity and the conservation of resources.
In this way, we seek to ensure a positive legacy for future generations. With this vision, we have developed
three fronts of action aligned with the Sustainable Development Goals:

Climate governance at Banco de Occidente is based on the recognition of the importance of climate change in our operations and in
our portfolio. We know that as a financial institution we have a great responsibility in managing decarbonization and climate resilience
throughout our value chain.
In the spirit of advancing these matters, we have held awareness-raising sessions, in collaboration with expert consultants, to train
our teams on the context of climate change, the recommendations of the TCFD framework, and the guidelines established by External
Circular No. 031 of 2021. Thus, we seek to impact our Board of Directors, the President, the Support Committees, the Managers, Directors
and employees of the Bank, including the Steering Committee of Fiduciaria de Occidente.
## Climate governancea
In order to guarantee the ownership of issues related to climate change, we have
three (3) lines of defense:
## First line of defense: Responsible for carrying out
actions in every process by following the Corporate
## Governance guidelines and the regulations in force
to prevent materialization of risks.
## Second line of defense: Comprised by the Risk Vice-
## Presidency and its managements, responsible for
evaluating, measuring, monitoring and mitigating
Third line of defense: Constitutes the Audit area,
whose purpose is to generate value aligned with
the Banks’ strategy through the organizational
## Climate governance

## Surveillance of Risks
President Audit Committee Comprehensive Risk Committee Sustainability

into all the Bank’s operations. These committees
are essential to ensure that ESG initiatives align with

and corporate responsibility in all our activities.
In 2023 the roles and responsibilities were established,
in 2024 we complemented the dissemination with
the periodicity of these committees. Some of the
roles and responsibilities, detailed below, will be
strengthened in 2025 with the aim of strengthening
our climate governance.

with the organization’s long-term goals. In addition,
it is important to note that this is an ongoing process,
involving periodic review and updating to adapt to
new challenges and opportunities that may arise in
the climate and risk management field.

## Environmental and Climate Change Policy of
## Banco de Occidente and Affiliates: In our policy we
establish the general principles that we must follow
to align and develop initiatives that respond to the
commitments of the sustainability strategy, in such a
way that they are oriented to the management of the
business around economic, social, environmental and
corporate governance matters. For us, it is essential
to consolidate sustainability and its articulation with
the purpose and business model of the Bank and its
affiliates. With this policy, we want to strengthen the
processes for the improvement of business operations,
organizational reputation and the positioning of the
organization and its affiliates as a benchmark in the
Colombian financial sector.
## Environmental principles
of environmental policy
and climate change:
1. S upport compliance with the environmental
regulations in force in the country and applicable
to our activities where Banco de Occidente and
its affiliates operate, as well as the voluntary
commitments that it adheres to.
2. D esign and implement a social and environmental
risk management system for projects financed
by Banco de Occidente and its financial
3. P romote financing for eco-efficient projects and
initiatives for both companies and individuals.
4. D evelop and strengthen green and/or blue
products and services for the different business
segments and measure their environmental
5. Implement an environmental management
system with responsible use and consumption
practices for resources such as energy, paper
and water and with performance objectives
6. P romote training and awareness among
employees, customers, suppliers and other
stakeholders about environmental care and
7. S trengthen initiatives with a high environmental
impact, such as our Planeta Azul Award
and Ecobooks, which exalt educational and
productive actions for the benefit of ecosystems
and natural resources.
8. P romote sustainable practices in our suppliers
and include these criteria in our evaluation and
selection processes for the purchase of goods
9. S upport environmental projects aligned with
our sustainability strategy, mainly those aimed
at energy transformation.
## Based on the sustainability strategy mentioned
above, we have developed different guidelines that
allow us to further ground the commitment we have
with future generations and with the environment,
which is why we have developed a specific strategy
that addresses mitigation and adaptation to climate
## Our purpose is to act sustainably, generating
awareness in all our employees and promoting
sustainable financing initiatives that contribute
to generating a positive impact on society, the
environment and business.
## Strategy covering climate change issues
At Banco de Occidente, we have implemented multiple initiatives that allow us to advance in the adoption
and appropriation of our climate strategy. It has different phases that include the recognition of risks derived
from climate change, the identification of decarbonization opportunities and the development of products
and services aligned with these findings. In this way, we contribute to nationally determined goals while
maintaining the Bank’s carbon neutrality and boosting climate resilience in our clients.
